on the householder's schedule, and this schedule differed fron1 the sehedule used

questions \Vere included either in the English or Scottish Householder's l{cturn
used at the censuses taken in Great Britain in 1921, and yielded n1ost valuable
results.
Following past practice the services of the State-controlled Constabulary .Force
were utilised in the delivery and collection of Census Returns, and it is evident that
it would have been difficult to obtain the services of a n1ore efficient and eonscient.ious
corp . .; of enurncrators.
For census purposes the country was diYidcd into 1,070
Enurneration Districts, for each of which a police constable or sergeant "·as appointed
to act as Enurnerator. The Enumerators carried out their work under the dire<'tion
of their District Inspectors, who nurnbered thirty-four.
The Schedules were delivered to householders and other persons required to
n1ake returns during the \Veek commencing 12th April. Colle(·tion took place over
the ten days, April 19th to the 28th. Special Population Summaries, cornpiled by
the Enumerators, were received in the Census Office during the second week in :\lay,
and frorn these a statement showing n1alc and fen1ale population by counties and
county boroughs was prepared, and furnished to ·Parlian1ent by you on the 19th of
that n1onth. Since it was impossible, at that date, to apply any detailed check to
the Enun1erators' special summaries, it is gratifying to note that the differcnees
between the figures given in the statetnent published on the 1 !Hh )fay and those
contained in this Report arc so srnall as t.o he imn1aterial. This refteets eredit on
the Enumerators, whose accuracy and thoroughness wilL I hope, be further con-
firn1ed when the Census Returns have all been exatnined and the final census results
are ready for puolication.
The Superintendents of Enumeration were instructed to scrutinise the Sehedulcs
and Enumeration Books of their respective districts, and send them to the Census
Ofllcc not later than the 7th June. It was considered that, by allowing the Super-
intendents a n1onth in which to pcrfortn this work, n greater (h'grec of accuracy
\vould be ensured in this Preliminary Report, and rnuch of the correspondence
regarding errors subsequently discovered at the Census Office would he avoided.
This Report is based upon the Abstracts compiled by Enunterators from their
Enumeration Books.
These Abstracts have all been earefully examined in the
Census Office, but as there has as yet been no opportunity for d{ecking the contents
of the Enumeration Books themselves, or examining the Census Returns, the figures
in this H.eport, though substantially correct, must be regarded as provisional and
subject to amendment when the main Census Reports arc published.
The figures contained in this Report relate only to population, habitable build-
ings (among which have been included all habitable premises, (•ara\·ans, and vessels),
.accommodation, families, and persons of 70 years of age and over. Fan1ilies may
be defined as "'separate occupations"; in the majority of eases these are households,
.and may include visitors, servants, and boarders.
Lodgers oecupying separate
accommodation are regarded as separate fan1ilics.
All area figures shown in the
tables are exclusive of the larger rivers, lakes, and tidcways (i.e., those not enclosed
·within townland boundaries); of land amounting to 2,29!) acres on the shores of
Laughs Erne, Castlehulme, and l\lacnean; and of ;) acres, the area of the 1\Iaiden's
Islands in the North Channel.
\Vork on the preparation of the detailed Census Reports. which will be compiled
from the Census Returns themselves, is now in progress. These H.eports will probably
con1prise a volume relating to each of the Six Counties anu Belfast County Borough,
a General l{eport for the whole country, and a Topographical Index to certain of
the tables in the County Volumes. The schen1c of tabulation to he ernployed in
preparing these reports involves the examination of each Census Return; the
classification of each entry on the returns, and allotting to each entry the appro-
priate code number; punching a card \vith the full particulars in respect of each
person; and, finally, the mechanieal tabulation of the particulars contained on the
cards. By utilising machine tabulators a great deal of labour will be saved, since
tables relating to several different subjects can he prepared rnerely by passing the
cards through the machines the requisite number of times.


---

## Page 5

111
The detailed Census Reports will contain tables dealing with the following
subjects:-
( l) Population by sex and ages, in administrative areas;
(2) Marital condition, orphanhood, and dependent children;
(3) Birthplace and nationality;
( 4) Religion ;
( 5) Class of school attended;
( 6) Occupation followed and industry in which engaged;
(7) National Health Insurance;
( 8) Infirmities;
(9) Houses, families, and accommodation.
While it is not yet possible to say when the detailed Reports will he ready, all
endeavours will be made to complete them at the earliest possible date, and as soon
as each Yolume is completed it will be submitte9. to you for presentation to Parlia-
ment.
It \Vas considered of first importance that this Report should be published
with the least possible delay, and the Statistical Notes contained in Part II arc
therefore brief, and deal only \vith the more outstanding general features disclosed
by the tables.
In conclusion, I wish to record my appreciation of the valuable assistance given
by the Press, the British Broadcasting Company, School Teachers, and others, in
endeavouring to familiarise the public with the requirements of the Census.
I have the honour to be,
Sir,
Your obedient Servant,
L. A. BULLWINKLE,
Registrar-General of Births, Deaths, and
1U arriages for Northern Ireland.

PART !I.-STATISTICAL NOTES.
Population of Northern lreland.-The total population in X orthern Ireland on
the night of 18th April, 1926, numbered, according to the Enun1erators' Abstracts,

    1,256,322 persons; 608,205 being males and 6-t8, 117 fern ales. In Table I the 1926
figures for the \vhole area are shown in comparison with those recorded at previous
censuses. It \vill be seen from this table that the steady decline in population fron1

    1841 onwards 'vas arrested during the decennium 1891--1!)01, and replaced by a
slight tendency to increase.
The increase in population over the past L3 years,
ho,vever, is very small, representing only about 0.5 per cent. of the total population.
The following table analyses the intercensal increases of the population:--

It will be seen from this Table, if \Ve exclude consideration of War deaths, that,
although there has been a considerable fall in the average annual nun1ber of both
births and deaths, the average annual excess of births over deaths during the period
1911-1926 is very little less than that for the previous decenniun1.
~Ioreover, it
is apparent that the increase that might have been expected from the excess of
births over deaths was almost wholly offset by emigration, including move1nent to
other parts of the British Isles, in the period 1891-1901, and in the last two
intercensal periods the net effect of migration has been an outward moven1ent,
numerically equivalent to about 83 and 94 per cent. respectively of the excess of
births over deaths.
In the intercensal period no\v under review-i.e., 1911-1926-the balance of
movetnent of population, after allowing for the effect of natural increase (i.e., excess
of births over deaths) as reduced by migration, is further reduced by the losses
'vhich occurred overseas during the War. In this connection it is probably as well
to point out that, whilst· average annual figures of births, deaths, and n1igration
have been employed in framing these comments, the disturbances caused by the
War in all of these factors were such as to render any attcn1pt to deduce argutnents
from the course of events over the past fifteen years one of considerable difficulty
in the absence of a very careful study of the detailed figures for each year.
Population and Variations in Population in Administrative Divisions of the Country.-
Table II sets out the population of each county (as constituted at the date of each
census) in 1926, in comparison with the population at previous censuses.
The increase of 5, 791 in total population is seen to be the net result of increases
in Belfast County Borough and County Down, set against decreases in each of the
remaining counties. It 'vill be noted that the trend of population in every county
but l)own has been uniformly in the same direction since 1841, the county named
providing, in 1926, the first recorded instance of an increase outside Belfast. The
increase in population in Belfast County Borough over the past fifteen years n1ay
in part be due to movement into the city from other counties, but it is clear that
any such movement has been more than offset by emigration from and other nloYe-
ment out of Belfast, since the increase in the population of Belfast falls short of
the excess of births over deaths (excluding War deaths overseas) recorded for that
area during the period by 28,044 persons.
------------- ---------- -----------------------------
• Including the deaths of non-civilians of Northern Ireland which occurred outside the country. It has not been
found possible to estimate the number of these deaths at all closely, but from the War :\Iortality lists of soldiers from
Ireland, which were compiled by the \Yar Office from Regimental Records, it appears that about 10,000 deaths of non-
civilians of Northern Ireland occurred outside the country during the last intercensal period, exclusive of the deaths of
regular soldiers stationed outside the country at the date of the 1911 Census.

## Page 8

2
The greatest absolute and percentage decreases in population occurred in the
Counties of Armagh and Tyrone, and the rate of decrease shown in County Fer-
managh is also relatively high.
Further reference to the Table reveals the fact that the present populations of
Counties Armagh, Fermanagh, and Tyrone are less than one-half those recorded
for the san1e counties in 1841, while the population of Belfast County Borough is
no-w nearly six times as great as it was in that year. It is to be noted, however,
that the boundaries of both County Arn1agh and Belfast County Borough have
been altered during the period.
Urban and Rural Districts.-From Table B below it will be seen that, of the
urban and rural areas into which Northern Ireland is divided for administrative
purposes, the total population of the County Boroughs and Urban Districts is no'v
slightly in excess of that in the Rural Districts, the Urban population ha'\;ying

    increased during the past 25 years from 42.9 to 50.7 per cent. of the total population.
When considering the relation between Urban and Rural populations it should,
however, be borne in mind that approximately 72 per cent. of the Urban population
is resident in Belfast and Londonderry County Boroughs, and a further 18 per cent.
concentrated in the seven largest Urban Districts, leaving only 15 per cent. (or
93,000 persons) distributed among the remaining 24 lJrban Districts.

The present population of County Boroughs and Urban Districts represents an
increase of 50,811 persons over the population of the same classes of area as consti-
tuted in 1911. Reference to Table Ilia, page 8, shows, however, that the actual
net increase within the areas covered by the County Boroughs and Urban Districts
as now constituted was 43,596 persons. The difference of 7,215 persons represents
the 1911 population of those portions of Rural Districts which have been added to
Urban Districts, or out of which new Urban Districts have since been created.
The foregoing comments on the trend of the population in Urban and Rural
Districts may SJ.Iggest that the Urban population has increased wholly at the expense
of the Rural population. Further reference to Table A, however, makes it difficult
to resist the conclusion that the decrease in Rural population is due more to
emigration than to movement within the country.
Tables III a and V both give population figures for each County, County Borough,
Urban District, and Rural District, the earlier census figures being adjusted where
necessary in order to correspond with the areas as constituted at the census
of 1926.


---

## Page 9

3
The net increase in Urban population of 43,596 persons comprises increases in

    Belfast of 28,060 persons, in Londonderry of 4,384 persons, and in 21 Urban Districts
of 12,046 persons, against which are set decreases amounting to 894 persons in the
remaining 10 Urban Districts.
The decrease of 37,805 persons in the population of the 32 Rural Districts com-
prises decreases amounting to 44,156 persons in 24 districts, and increases totalling
6,351 persons in the remaining 8 districts.
Of the 23 urban areas which have gained in population during the past 15
years, 9 show increases of over 10 per cent., the most remarkable growth being
in Bangor Urban District~ where the population has increased by 65.4 per cent.
The area of this district has been enlarged since 1911, but it is estimated that the
1911 population of the added area amounted only to 275 persons. The greatest
percentage decreases in Urban Districts are between 6 and 7 per cent., and occur
in the two least populous areas of this type, Tanderagee and Keady.
Of the 24 Rural Districts showing decreases in population during the last 15
years, 6 have suffered a loss of more than 10 per cent., the greatest percentage
decrease (nearly 16 per cent.) occurring in Tanderagee, the least populous Rural
District. The greatest absolute decrease ( 4,042 persons) occurred in Newry No. 2
Rural District, the present population of which is 26,349 persons. Belfast Rural
District shows an increase of over 12 per cent., whilst Castlereagh Rural District,
'vith a population of 7 ,298, sho,vs an increase of over 40 per cent. These increases,
and indeed all increases and decreases in Urban and Rural Districts within easy
access of Belfast, should be considered in conjunction 'vith the trend of the popula-
tion of the County Borough itself, when forming an opinion of the extent to which
the activities of the city have grown, as measured by the increase in population
since 1911.
From Table IIIb it 'viii be seen that of the four towns created under the Towns
Improvement (Ireland) Act, 1854, and not since constituted Urban Districts~ the
population of Whitehead alone has increased during the period 1911-1926. This
to,vn, however, was only constituted as such in April, 1925. The population of each
of the remaining three towns-vi7.., Antrim, Aughnacloy, and Gilford-has decreased
considerably. A notable feature of this table is the increase in population shown in
Whiteabbey, mainly due to the completion of t'vo Sailors' and Soldiers' !lousing
Schemes at that place.
Table V gives population figures for each Urban and Rural District, and for
each Ward of Belfast and Londonderry County Boroughs. Of the Wards of Belfast
and Londonderry it appears that the greatest absolute increase in population
occurred in the Ormeau VVard of Belfast; whilst the greatest percentage increases
occurred in East "\V ard, Londonderry, and Falls and Victoria Wards, Belfast.
Sexes.-Table I shows that whilst there are approximately 40,000 more females
than males in Northern Ireland (the ratio being 1,066 to 1,000), the preponderance
of females has been falling since 1881. The fall was not so rapid in the past 15 years
as in the previous decennium,. a fact which to some extent may be accounted for
by the loss of male population during the War; but the general tendency is indicated
by the circumstance that the increase in total population has, despite the loss in
man-power referred to, been almost wholly in the males.
Table C (page 4)
shows that in Urban Districts as a whole there is a marked preponderance of females,
and in Rural Districts as a whole, a very slight preponderance of males. In both
classes of area there has been an appreciable decrease in the number of females, in
relation to the number of males, since 1911. It is noteworthv that the six Urban
Districts 'vhich contain the highest proportion of females to males in the entire
territory are also popular seaside resorts.
Density of Population per Acre, Habitable Buildings, and Accommodation.-Tables VI,
VII, and VIII refer to Habitable Buildings. This term denotes :-
(a) Each structure (excepting institutions) which stands alone, or is separated
from another by a party wall, and which is used or intended partly
or wholly for habitation;
(b) Each institution, regardless of the number of buildings within its precincts;
(c) Each vessel, caravan, tent, or other movable dwelling.
This definition does not, however, apply to the last three columns of Table VI,
which are strictly limited to the classes of building noted in the heading to those
columns. Uninhabited buildings include premises which, though furnished for
occupation, were not in fact occupied on Census night.

Persons per Acre.-The density of population, as n1ight have been expected,
is highest in Belfast, and, within Belfast, highest in Court Ward, where there are
189 persons to the acre. \Vhen considering the figures as to density in a particular
area (Table VIa) it must be recognised that the distribution of the population is
not necessarily uniforn1.
.A. n1arked feature of the density factor is the very wide
variation in Urban l)istricts, ranging fron1 32.1 in Carrickfergus to 1.3 in Portste,vart.
Habitable Buildings.-Table VIII shows that the nun1 her of completed habitable
buildings in Xorthern Ireland has decreased since 1901, whereas the number of
inhabited buildings has increased, thus reducing the margin available for occu-
pation frorn about 11 per cent. in 1901 to 6 per cent. at the present time. The
number of houses in course of erection at the date of the census 'vas, however,
greater than any previously recorded.
Table VII shows the changes which have taken place in the number of habitable
buildings between 1911 and 1926, the percentages given in the last column affording
an interesting comparison with those appearing in the last column of Table II. At
first sight this comparison suggests that the need for houses is greatest in County
})own. In this case, howcYcr~ it is apparent that there was a relatively high number
of uninhabited houses in 1911 which, presumably, to son1e extent, have n1et the


---

## Page 11

5
need. Another feature brought out by the Table is that building is now proceeding
to a much greater extent in every county than in 1911.
Tables VIa and -VIb shovv that some building activity is in progress in almost
every Urban and Rural District, and that the number of houses under construction
in Rural J)istricts exceeds that in Urban Districts and County Boroughs.
Accommodation.-Tables VIa and VIb sho\v that there is, on the whole, about
one room per unit of the population, the variation ranging between .65 (in West
Ward, Londonderry) and 1.9 (in Portste,vart Urban District). The differences are
most marked when considering holiday resorts \vith acco1nmodation for visitors,
and also \vhen comparing the several Wards of the County Boroughs. The question
of overcrowding in the County Boroughs has, to some extent, been illustrated
separately in Tables IX and X. The tenements of one room dealt with in these
tables were each occupied by one family as enun1erated for census purposes. In
the case of Belfast there has been more than a sixfold increase in the number
of tenements of one room since 1911, whilst in Londonderry the number has been
nearly doubled. Relatively to population, ho\vever, the position in Londonderry
seems to be far more acute. In Belfast over 7,000 people are living in single-roomed
tenements, and nearly 3,000 in Londonderry are similarly placed.
Population Aged 70 Years and Over.-Table D belo'v reveals an interesting
feature in the relatively high proportion of persons aged 70 and over as recorded in
1911. It has been suggested that the widespread use of earlier Census Returns to
provide evidence of age in Old Age Pension cases, in the absence of birth registration,
produced a tendency in persons approaching 70 years of age at the time of the 1911
census to overstate their ages. The evidence in this matter, however, is by no means
conclusive, and there is good reason to believe that other factors also were operating
to cause the phenomenon in question. In so far, however, as a tendency to over-
statement did arise from the methods employed to secure evidence of age, the
circumstance is one which serves to emphasise the necessity for treating the par-
ticulars to be obtained from Census Schedules merely upon a statistical basis, and
for no purpose connected \vith the individual in respect of whom the return is made.

(b) The boundaries of the Counties of Antrim, Armagh, and Down were altered pursuant to the Local
Government (Ireland) Act, 1898.
(c) The boundary of the City of Belfast was extended by the Belfast Corporation Act, 1896, whereby
portions of the Counties of Antrim and Down were transferred to the city. By virtue of the
Local Government (Ireland) Act, 1898, the City of Belfast became a County Borough.
(d) The boundaries of the Counties of Antrim and Londonderry were altered by the Local Government
Board (Ireland) Provisional Order Confirmation (No. 3) Act, 1914, consequent on the extension
of Portrush Urban District.

(a) Portrush Vrban District was extended on 1st April, 1915, by the addition thereto of portions of the
Rural Districts of Ballymoney and Coleraine. The portion of the latter Rural District was at
the same time transferred from County Londonderry to County Antrim.
(b) Ballycastle l;rban District was created on 1st April, 1921, and comprises the townland of Townparks,
which was formerly included in Ballycastlc H.ural District.
(c) Aghalee Hural District was abolished and its area added to Lisburn Hural District on 1st April, 1920.
(d) Crossmaglen Rural District was abolished and its area added to ~cwry ~o. 2 Rural District on
1st April, 1921.
(e) Bangor Urban District was extended on 1st June, 1924, hy the transfer thereto of a portion of
~ewtownards Rural Di~trict, the population of which, in 19ll, is estimated to have been 275.
This figure has been used in adjusting the 1911 populations of Bangor Urban Distriet and
~ewtownards Hural District.

NoTE.-The County Constituencies are comprised of the Counties named in the table. East Belfast
consists of Pottinger, Dock, and Victoria \Yards; North Belfast consists of Duncairn, Shankill,
and Clifton \Vards; South Belfast consi<sts of Cromac, \Vindsor, and Ormeau Wards; and \Vest
Belfast consists of Falls, Smithfield, St. Anne's, St. George's, Court, and Woodvale Wards.

ULSTER YEAR BOOK, 1926.

THIS YEAR BooK presents, in a simple, conYenient, and accessible form, statistical a11<l other information regarding
Northern Ireland and its people; their environment and soeial conditions, health and education, transport and emn-
munication, agriculture, trade, and industry.
Official statistics relating to ::\orthem Ireland must be sought in a variety of departmental reports and publit·ations.
In this Year Book the aim has been to bring together the more important otlicial statisties of Xorthcrn Ireland, arranw·
them in a simple manner, and pres<'nt them within the limits of a yo!UillL' of r<'asonahlc size.
The authenticity of the statistit·s, whieh became available during 1 !1:!;), i-.; \"(Jtwhed for by the Departments and
Ministries eoneerned.
The Year Book contains an Ordnance Survey )lap of Xorthern Ireland aud 7 plates and diagrams.

## Page 27

iii.
PREFACE.
This volume has been prepared in accordance with the provisions of Section 6
(1) of the Census Act (Northern Ireland), 1925. The 1926 Census statistics which
it contains were compiled from the returns made as at midnight of the 18-19th
April, 1926: they supersede those in the Preliminary Report, which was issued
in August, 1926, and may be regarded as final.
The Census publications consist of:-
1.
SEVEN COUNTY VOLUMES, including a volume relating to BELFAST COUNTY
BoROUGH. These volumes contain population and housing figures
for the smaller administrative areas including townlands ; and tables
dealing with ages and marital condition of the people, education,
occupations, industries, birthplaces, religious professions, orphanhood
and persons suffering from infirmities; together with statistical notes
on some of the more outstanding features disclosed by the tables.
2.
This GENERAL REPORT.
3.
A TOPOGRAPHICAL INDEX, dealing in separate alphabetical sections with
each type of area for which statistics are published in the County
series. The particulars given for each territo~al unit include references
to the 6" Ordnance Survey maps and to Tables 3, 4, or 5 of the relative
County Volume.
The net prices for the above publications are 5s. Od. for each County Volume
and lOs. Od. each for the General Report and the Topographical Index. A complete
set of these volumes may be obtained directly from. the Superintendent H.M.
Stationery Office, 15, Donegall Square West, Belfast, at the special inclusive price
of £2 2s. Od. strictly net.
The Registrar-General is empowered by Section 6 (2) of the Census Act
(Northern Ireland), 1925, to prepare and supply from the Census returns, statistics
other than those published in the Census Repor~s at the request and cost of any
local authority or person. Any special demands for statistics more detailed than
those published (either as regards subject matter or area) can thus, as far as office
records permit, be satisfied. Requests for special compilation of Census statistics
should be addressed to the Registrar-General, who will be pleased to advise and
furnish an estimate of cost.
April, 1929.
GENERAL 'REGISTER 'OFFICE'

## Page 33

ix.
PART I.
INTRODUCTION.
The first census of the population of Northern Ireland since that of 1911 was
taken on the 18th April, 1926. This volume gives an account of the methods
employed and contains statistical notes on the recorded results, together with
general tables and appendices. Explanatory notes and definitions of terms used
have been collected together and form Part II of this volume, references thereto
being inserted at the beginning of the tables where necessary.
(1) Historical Survey.-The first complete census of Ireland was taken in 1821,
and the population of Northern Ireland according to each of the decennial censuses
from that date to 1911 are given in Table 2 of this report in conjunction with the
figures for 1926.
While some doubts have been entertained as to the accuracy of

    the 1821 and 1831 census figures, the results of the census taken from 1841 onwards
are generally considere5Lto be substantially reliable. A complete account of the
earlier censuses of Irelana is given in the General Report on the Census of Ireland,
1901, and it is therefore not considered necessary to repeat this survey in the present
volume.
(2) Preparations for the Census.
(a) Census Committee.
The main work of preparing for and taking the Census commenced in the
summer of 1925 with the formation of a committee, comprising representatives
of each of the the 1v1inistries in N orthem Ireland, whose principal function was to
determine the nature and extent of the information to be sought from the public
on the Census forms.
During the autumn of 1925 the committee considered the
needs of each Ministry as put forward by its own representative on the Committee.
Eventually a form of schedule basically similar to the schedule used at the 1921
Census of England and Wales, but containing additional questions relating to
·Religion, School, National Health Insurance, and Infirmities, was agreed upon
and accordingly recommended.
(b) Legislation.
The recommendations of the Committee were adopted and are embraced in the
second Schedule to the Census Act (Northern Ireland), 1925, which received the
Royal Assent on the lOth November, 1925. Copies of this Act and the House-
holder's Return appear in Appendices A and B to this Report.
The Act provided that the head of every household, establishment, and
vessel, should be personally responsible for the filling up of the schedule delivered
to him by the Enumerator. Upon collection, the Enumerator was required to
complete such of the returns as should appear to him to be defective, and correct
such as he should find erroneous.
Previous Census Acts for Ireland placed upon
Enumerators the responsibility for filling up Schedules delivered to householders,
though actually the latter were requested to perform the task; and the Northern
Ireland Act thus effected a change in principle without altering in any appreciable
degree the practice which had hitherto prevailed.
The Act also provided that'' The Registrar-General, under the direction of the
Ministry of Finance, may collect and publish from time to time any available
statistical information concerning Northern Ireland until the next census is taken."
This provision, which had hitherto not been included in Irish Census Acts, supplied
the requisite authority for the publication of the Ulster Year Book.
'


---

## Page 34

x.
(c) Definition of Population.
The Census Act provided for the enumeration of all persons pr~sent ~n Northern
Ireland at midnight on the Census day and any persons vyho arnved 1n Northern
Ireland during the morning following Census day, prov1ded they had not been
enumerated elsewhere (i.e., in the Irish Free State). All persons were enumerated
in the areas in which they spent census night or at vvhich they arrived on the fol-
lovving morning. The population of each areas as given in the Census volumes
therefore is the population actually present in that area at the date of the Census.
This method of enumeration has always been follo\vedin Ireland and Great Britain,
and being simpler, is considered to ensure more reliable results than t~e method
adopted in various other countries (e.g.,France, Germany, Belgium and Switzerland),
by which all persons normally resident in an area are enumerated in that area
whether present or not at.the time when the census is taken. The census date and
hour were fixed vvith a view to ensuring that the proportion of the population
not at home should be at a minimum, and accordingly (with the exception of sn1all
areas containing institutions) the numerated population does not differ appre-
ciably from the normally resident population.
_ (d) Prepa'J'ations for Enumerat,z'on.
Following past practice the service of the State Constabulary were utilised in
the delivery and collection of Census Returns. Each of the thirty-four District
Inspectors of the Royal Ulster Constabulary \vas responsible for the enumeration
of his own area, and, as a first step, was required to divide it into a number of
enumeration districts and submit to this office a list of the contents of these,
together with boundary descriptions where necessary.
In order to ensure that
every townland and street in the country was accounted for, the lists ·were carefully
checked in the Census Office against Orders made from time to time by the Ministry
of Home Affairs, which defined the geographical constitution of various local
government areas, and street lists supplied by local authorities.
The mapping
of enumeration districts provided a further check on the completeness of the
arrangements for enumeration. The country was divided into 1,070 enumeration
districts, for each of which a police Constable or Sergeant was appointed to act as
enumerator.
The drafting and printing of Census schedules, enumeration books, books of
instructions to superintendents and enumerators, and the numerous other necessary
forms was carried out during the six months prior to Census day. :Details of the
duties of the enumerating staff will be found i.;n their instructions which have been
reprinted in Appendix C to this report.
(3) Enumeration.-The schedules were delivered to householders and other.
persons required to make returns during the week conimencing 12th April.
Col-
lection took place in urban areas over the week ended 24th April and in rural areas
over the ten days, April 19th to the 28th.
Population and Housing Sumn1aries
were completed by the Enumerators; and the schedules, enumeration books, and
other Census material.collected from them by the Superintendents on or before the
8th May. The schedules and enumeration books were then examined by the Super-
intendents to satisfy themselves that the instructions had been properly carried out
and all documents,· etc., were forwarded to the Census Office on or before the 15th
June.
Provision was made at this Census, for the first time, to enable persons, who
might be reluctant to disclose particulars to some other person required to make a
census return, to make a separate confidential return. The number of cases in
which persons claimed to be allowed to make such a confidential return was rela-
tively small. The particulars given on confidential returns were subsequently
added to the returns in which they normally would have been included, by the
Enumerators.
(4) Publicity.-This was secured through the medium of the Press, the British
B:roadcasting Company and posters displayed -at .Police Stations and Libraries.
In additjon, principal teachers in elementary schools explained the census schedule
to the senior classes.
·


---

## Page 35

xi .
. (5) Preliminary Reports.-From the Enun1erators' special population sum-
manes a statement showing the male and female population by counties and county
boroughs was prepared and furnished to Parliament on the 19th 1\:Iay; 1926.
A preliminary report relating to population, housing, room accommodation,
families, and persons over 70 years of age, vvas issued on the 2nd ... .1\ugust, 1926.
The total population of the country as given in the preliminary reports and this
the final report are as follows :-
Persons.
Males.
Females.
Statement of 19th May,
1926
1,255,881
608,396
647,485

1,256,322
608,205
648,117

1,256,561
608,088
648,473
There was, of course, no time to apply a detailed check to the special popu-
lation summaries, and the enumerators' abstracts from ·which the preliminary
report was prepared, while carefully checked in tb.e Census Office, could not in the
time available be checked against the actual census schedules. Nevertheless, the
differences between the preliminary and the final figures are so small as to be
immaterial. This, together with the fact that no complaints of non-enumeration
were re,ceived in the Census Office, reflects credit on the enumerators and superin-
tendents for the accuracy and thorouglmess :with which they carried out their work.
(6) Tabulation of Census Results
(a) Methods adopted.
The framework of all the main census tables having been drawn up, the question
of method of tabulation had to be decided.
The choice lay between hand tabulation
by a " ticking " process and the use of sorting and counting machines to deal vvith
cards containing punched particulars in code form.
The former method has
hitherto been used for Irish Censuses, but the limitations of this method are such
that only simple statistical tables can be economically produced by its means.
Careful consideration of estimated time and costs and the experience of the England
and vVales Census Authorities, who used the latter method at their Censuses of
1911 and 1921, made it abundantly clear that all the tabulation, excepting that of
the housing and accommodation particulars and the populations of small areas,
could be carried out more quickly, accurately, and cheaply by using modern machine
methods:
The relatively small portion of tabulation work which was not
dealt with by the machines was done by the ticking and scheduling process.
(b) Coding particulars on Schedules.
A necessary preliminary to machine tabulation was the coding of particulars
contained in the Census schedules, i.e., reducing each verbal statement to a reference
number signifying a heading in a code. This work was carried out by a temporary
male staff who were divided into sections operating on the various_ columns in the
schedule as follows :-
Coding Sections.

Birthplace and Nationality

## Page 36

xii.
The percentage check applied to each coder's work \Vas regulated weekly
by the percentage of errors in his previous work as discl~s~d by checking .. In
addition to the coding staff a section \vas engaged upon examm1ng the enumeration
books and schedules and comparing one \vith the other, processes necessary to ensl?-re
that the schedules were in a proper form to be operated upon by the machine
section.
The work of coding and checking, and examining enumeration books and

    schedules \vas commenced on the 14th July, 1926, and con1pleted on the 26th March,
1927, the staff for this purpose comprising temporary male clerks under the super-
vision of three clerical officers.
(c) Machine Card.
The census particulars from the coded schedules were transferred to machine
cards, one card being used for each person in the country. The following is a copy
of the card used, the columns on which will be seen to correspond to the information
given on the schedule. The actual cards used n1easured 7 i ins. by 3! ins.

The code nun1bers are represented on the card by holes punched as illustrated.
Column 10 of the card provided for both the "county" code and the "under 12"
and " 12 and over " age division code ; column 21 covered the orphanhQod as well
as the marital condition codes ; and columns 11 to 14 were not used. The X and Y
in some columns formed part of the code, and, in addition, the punching of an X
operated a device for skipping the remainder of the field in which it occurred.
For example, in punching a card for a child, where there are no particulars to
punch in the occupation, industry, and dependency columns, time is saved by punch-
ing X in column 31 and then skipping immediately to column 39, and similarly
skipping to the end of the card after punching X in column 40.
(d) Programme of Machine Operations.
It was of extreme importance that all unnecessary operations should be
eliminated and the total number of cr card passages" through the machines reduced
to a minimum. It was also essential that the machine operations should be so
arranged that the sorting and counting machines could work concurrently without
breaks caused by r'waiting" on each other.
A programme was, therefore, prepared in
consultation with experts. This programme set forth the whole of the sorting and


---

## Page 37

xiii.
counting work in 79 separate sections or processes, each of which was suitable for
handing over to one machine operator. Detailed working instructions regarding
each process were also prepared for the guidance of the operators.
The total
number of card passages through the machines required by this programme \vas
12,713,834 on the sorting machines and 7,308,120 on the counter, ·which is equiva-
lent to the passage of each of the million and a quarter cards through a sorting
machine ten times and through the counter six times. It \Vas found that some
of the. simpler sorting could be done more quickly by a hand" needling" process.
The equivalent of 9,281,846 additional cards vvas dealt with in this ·way. It is
interesting to note that a complete sort into numerical order on a two figure code
column, for example, necessitates the passage of all the cards through the sorting
machine twice.
(e) Card Punching.
The punching machines are of two kinds: key punches for punching columns
15-45 on one card at a time and a gang punch for punching batches of cards in the
first ten columns in one operation, where the numbers in these columns are common
to many cards. Tlus ·work was done by seven young girls and a supervisor in the
seven months October, 1926, to April, 1927. The average rate of key punching

    was 239 cards an hour and gang punching 3, 718 cards an hour.
62 per cent. of
the punching was checked on special check punching machines. The effect of
punching is that each digit of the code numbers is punched out in the appropriate
column on the card. For example, age 27 is indicated on a card by punching out
the 2 in column 22 and the 7 in column 23. It is through these punched holes that
the electrical brushes of the sorting and counting machines make contact and so
operate these machines.
(f) Sorting and Counting.
Two sorting machines, one with a counting attachment, and one three-bank ·
counter (i.e., a machine which counts on three columns of the card at one time)
were used for this work. The first sorter commence:l working on 3rd January

    1927, the other two machines on 17th January, 1927, and the work was completed
on 15th October, 1927. Female staff was also engaged on this ·work and comprised
one general supervisor, two operators on the counter, one on each sorter, and one
relief operator who was mainly engaged on hand sorting, assembling cards and
labelling boxes of cards with special labels to show the contents of the boxes,
arrangement of the cards, and processes completed.
·
The first process was a sort into 1nales and females in each enumeration area.
The tabulated results of this sort \Vere compared \Vith hand tabulated enumeration
book figures.
Differences were reconciled and thereafter these verification figures
were used as a standard on which checks ·were applied at every possible stage of
the work. The earlier processes provided the figures for tables dealing with the
smaller areas and as these were completed the cards were by stages amalgamated
until the later processes which dealt ·with tables for the whole country were reached
Cards for males and females were dealt with separately throughout, and all results
were entered on tabulation sheets specially prepared to facilitate the compilation
of the published tables.
(7) Publication.-The first ·of the final census reports to be published was that
for Belfast County Borough which was issued on the 5th March, 1928. Following
this at average intervals of three weeks reports were published for each of the six
counties, Tyrone County Volume, the last, being published on th.e 6th July, 1928.

    In a11, the County Volumes contained 590 foolscap pages, of which 429 pages were
occupied by the 1926 Census abstract tables and 161 by statistical notes, including
comparative tables.
A Topographical Index to areas for which statistics were published in the
County Volumes is now nearing completion, and will comprise about 170 foolscap
pages.
Besides references to the relative tables in the County Volumes, this Index
will include references to the 6 inch Ordnance Survey Map sheets on which each
area is to be found. The preface to the Index will contain a b1ief historical survey
and descriptions of the several administrative areas into which the country is divided ..

(10) Scope and Frequency of Census Enumerations.-The series of decennial
censuses which have heretofore b~en taken in Ireland ending with that in 1911 have
been designed to collect what may be regarded as something approaching the maxi-
mum amount of information which can be secured in such an operation. Following
the lapse of fifteen years since 1911, when it first became possible to take a census in
1'{9rthern Ireland, a full census has again been taken. It differed from the 1911


---

## Page 39

XV
census by the exclusion of questions relating to u Education " (( Particulars as to
Marri~ge," " I?sh la~,guage " and various subsidiary inqui1ies, but included
quest1ons relatmg to
Orphanbocd," "Nationality," "School," "Employer's
Nm.~.e. and ~usiness," '' Nati?nal Healih Insurance" and "Number and ages of
all_ hv1ng children _and st,~p-c-hJldren under I6 years of age in respect of l\farried 1\fen,
Widowers and Widows.
The 1926 census In Northern Ireland \vas thus brought
n1ore into line with the decennial censuses taken in Great Britain.
In considering the course to be adopted for future census enumerations
in Northern Ireland it may be argued that there is no case for further action until
the lapse of another 15 years \vhen a fu1iher full census could be taken at a date
corresponding to that adopted for the usual decennial census in Great Britain.
When considering the results of the present census, ho\vever, one cannot a void
being impressed by the degree of misstatement which appears to have been shown
in the matter of the recorded ages of persons and in this alone the position is infinitely
:vorse than in Great Blitain. Apart from any other reasons \vhich may arise therefore
It. seems that real advantages might be secured in familialising the population
with the process of census taking, etc., if simple enumeration of the population,
coupled ·with a request for the minimu1n of information, such as age and birthplace,
were resorted to at more frequent intervals. In comparison with the cost of a
full census such enumerations \vould be relatively inexpensive. In the absence of
any such action designed to secure greater precision in the ans\vering of questions
the advantages \vhich might appear to be secured from a full census at a some\vhat
remote date n1ust prove to son1e extent illusory.
(II) Form of Reports.-Whilst it is possible to report upon the process of census
taking, the essence of such a report being contained in this introduction, the material
collected as a result of the census does not lend itself to the same continuity of treat-
ment or lead by itself to such informative conclusions as might result from the
comprehensive collection of data in relation to and preparation of a report upon
a specific subject. The results of the census must unavoidably be recorded very
much in the form of crude statistical data items of \Vhich may be of value in relation
to any number of inquiries which may be instituted by the Government or the
PubHc.
Figures in respect of housing, for exan1ple, place each Local i\.uthority
in possession of data which, whilst not providing a complete picture of the position
in its area, should nevertheless prove of value in any consideration given to this
question.
Under the conditions described, the manner. in which the census results are
presented is largely dictated by reasons of expediency. It would have been possible
to provide separate volumes for each class of mate1ial, such as "Occupations,"
"Religions," "Birthplaces," etc., for the country as a \vhole, in which case it
would be necessary to select material from each such volume in order to obtain the
complete range of data relating to a particular county. The course followed has ..
however, been largely determined by precedent, an'd the method adopted in this
matter by England and Wales.
County volumes have been provided, and what is
termed ;a General Report. As previously explained, the latter does not lend
itself to treatment as a continuous narrative and hence consists mainly of statistical
notes followed by tables summarising the main statistics for the country as a whole.
In the process of preparing the statistical notes some consideration has been
given to the question of the relation of census statistics to those compiled by other
Administrative Departments and certain points of value for departmental purposes
have emerged. The full value of the census material cannot be realised, however,
unless relevant sections of it are made the basis for the study of a particular subject,
statistics from other sources being employed in conjunction \vith the census material
for such purposes. Subjects such as the rates of mortality from various causes in
relation to the position in other countries; the economic structure of Northern
Ireland in relation to Great Britain; the economic effect of tuberculosis and the
progress recorded in dealing with this disease, etc., suggest themselves as suitable
for examination and report. The extent·to \vhich the census material is used in this
way depends very much upon the Public demand for information on subjects such
as those jndicated, aJ;,~.d, failing this, it must be left to posterity to devise a system
by which the historical statistical records can be made to play their part in the
progress and \velfare of the community.

## Page 40

xvi
(12) Conclusion.-! t will be appreciated that the work of census taking,
together with t:he production of the published reports, is a task of considerable
magnitude which could not have been effectively carried out without the co-operation
of the various Ministries.
In particular the Royal Ulster Constabulary, which
provided the corps of enumerators carried the main burden of the work of census
taking. The Ordnance Survey Department contributed in the preparation of
maps and the determination of areas and the Valuation Department supplied
appropriate figures for each area, in conjunction with Ordnance Survey.
Special mention must be made of the assistance rendered to this Office by the
General Register Office, London, without whose aid it would not be too much to
say that the work of census taking in Northern Ireland would have proved far more
costly and laborious. The Registrar General for Scotland and the Director of
Statistics for the Irish Free State have also provided valuable assistance in any
matter in which their aid has been sought, and the Imperial Stationery Office have
been responsible for arranging the printing of' the very considerable volume of
material required.

PART. II.
EXPLANATORY NOTES AND DEFINITIONS OF
TERMS USED IN THIS VOLUME.
NOTE :-In Parts II and III of this report the present tense has generally been used in referring to
facts and figures relating to the date of the Census, i.e. 18th April, 1926.
AREA.
The acreages given are as measured by the Ordnance Survey of 1899-1907.
They exclude-
In Tables I, ·3 and 4
(a) foreshore and unnamed islands of less than one
In Tables 3 and 4
rood in extent;
(b) Carlingford and Strangford Loughs ;
(c) The Maiden's Islands in the North Channel;
(d) certain lands on shore of Newry river ;
(e) land on shores of Loughs Erne, Castlehulme, and
Macnean;
(f) the larger rivers, lakes, and tideways.
N.B.-While (c)-(f) above are not enclosed within to\vnland boundaries,
they form part of administrative areas. The extent of these excluded portions
is shown in footnotes to Tables 3 and 4.
POPULATION.
The population comprises all persons, including Forces of the Crown,

    enumerated as at midnight of Sunday the 18th-19th April, 1926, together with
those who, not having been enumerated elsewhere, arrived in port or were rec~ived
into any dwelling on the morning of the 19th April before the Census returns were
collected.
The population assigned to each area is that actually present (as defined
above) within it, whether as permanent residents or temporary visitors, persons
in port but on ship board being classified to the adjacent area.
In general these definitions apply to the population as enumerated at previous
censuses, and shown in Table 2.
ADMINISTRATIVE DIVISIONS.
N orthem Ireland comprises six counties and two county boroughs. The

    counties are divided into 63 county districts (31 urban and 32 rural) and sub-
divided into 681 district electoral divisions, 65 of which are situate in urban dis-
tricts. Of this latter group 49 are coterrrrinous with wards, and one js divided into
two wards. The counties are are also divided into 129 county electoral divisions.
The two county boroughs are divided into fifteen wards and five wards re-
spectively, each of which is coterminous with a district electoral division.
Twenty-seven poor law unions are contained within Northern Ireland. Twenty
of these are situate wholly in one county, four extend into two counties, and one
into three counties. Of the remaining two, one lies partly in a county and partly
in a county borough, the other partly in two counties and partly in a county borough.
The medical dispensary districts situate in N orthem Ireland number 178.
Poor
law unions and medical dispensary rustricts are, respectively, superintendent
registrars) and registrars' districts for the registration of marriages, births, and
deaths.

PARLIAMENTARY CONSTITUENCIES.
The present parliamentary constituencies., numbering nine, were created by
the Government of Ireland Act, 1920. Belfast County Borough is divided into four
constituencies, viz., East Belfast, North Belfast, South Belfast, and West Belfast ;
and the remaining five are composed of the administrative counties of Antrim,
Armagh, Down, Fermanagh-Tyrone, and Londonderry (with the county borough).
HOUSES.
I. The term (( House " denotes
(a) each structure (except institutions) which stands alone or is separated
from another by a vertical party wall; and which is used or intended
partly or wholly for habitation ;
(b) each institution (viz., hospital, sanatorium, nursing home, infirmary,
workhouse, asylum} religious or charitable community, residential
school or college, barracks or other premises under Naval, Military,
Air Force, or Police discipline, prison or other place of detention),
regardless of the number of buildings within its precincts;
(c) each vessel, caravan, tent, or other movable dwelling.
The 1911 figures for " Houses '' do not include vessels, and each separate
building within the precincts of an institution was at that Census regarded as a
separate tl House."
2.
The terms " Boarding House " and " Lodging House " denote, in addition
to habitations so described, any private house containing three or more boarders
or lodgers.
3.
The term (( Private House " denotes all habitations other than
(a)
dwellings within the precincts of an institution;
(b)
hotels, boarding houses, and other such communal dwellings;
(c)
(d)
(e)
(f)
(g)
premises in which three or more boarders, lodgers, or trade assistants
reside;
residences occupied by virtue of the tenant's employment (except rectories
and manses, and dwellings belonging to but detached from domestic
establishments) ;
premises occupied by caretakers ;
ships, caravans, tents, and other moveable dwellings ;
barns, stables, and out-houses occupied on Census night.
The term includes dwellings over shops except when these are occupied as
described at (c) and (d) above.
4. The term "Inhabited" denotes actual residence of a person or persons,
on Census night.
H Uninhabited" houses, therefore, include those temporarily
vacant through the absence of the usual occupiers as well as those without tenants.
Enumerators were instructed to omit from their enumeration empty houses in such
an advanced state of decay that there was little likelihood that they would again
be made fit for habitation. In this matter they were required to use their own
judgment.
ROOMS.
I. The rooms enumerated are the usual living rooms, including bedrooms
and kitchens, but excluding sculleries (i.e., rooms used only for washing, cleaning,
01 " .. :;~ing), pantries, cloakrooms, landings, closets or bathrooms, or any ware-
house, ofi·~~e or shop rooms.

clude secondary schools not recognised by the Ministry of Education,
universities,

commercial schools, home or private tuition, etc.

## Page 45

xxi.
OCCUPATIONS AND INDUSTRIES.
The economic. activity of the population may be regarded from two distinct
view points, namely, that of occupation and that of industry. The occupation
indicates the class of work performed by an individual, while -the industrv shows
the position of the worker in the economic structure of the countrv. The ~basis of
the latter is the firm, establishment, undertaking, or service with v~~hich the worker
is connected, and \vithin this group, categories of \vorkers of various occupations
are employed. Logically this definition covers all those \Vives and other members
of a family engaged in " home duties " \Vho render services for which no cash pay-:-
ment is usually n1ade, but for Census purposes these are excluded from considera-
tion, and the term "occupied" is generally limited to those "gainfully occupied,"
but includes persons vvho were out of work as \Veil as those who were in employment
at the date of the Census.
The fact that occupations are personal and based on the individual worker,
and that industries are based on the plant or establishment, implies that occupations
exist within industries and not vice versa. It is true that certain occupations
exist in only one industry (for example, farmers in the industry of agriculture),
but a number of the chief occupations are found in most industries. It is therefore
essential in dealing with occupations and industries to consider them independently
and use two distinct classifications.
For the first time in this country this has been
done, previous censuses having considered the economic activity of the population
from the "occupations" point of view only.
The classifications of occupations which alone were used in 1911 and at earlier
censuses were only in part occupational, being largely industrial in nature. Workers
in the professions and the services and in certain numerically important occupations,
as clerks, carters, engine drivers, commercial travellers, common to many industries,
were distinguished occupationally, but in a large proportion of cases the distinction
was industrial rather than occupational, such headings as rope, twine, cord-maker,
dealer; cotton, cotton goods-manufacture ; gas works service, being very common.
Such industrial groups as these afford no information as to the nature of the occu-
pation of the individual concerned, who in the last mentioned case might be a gas
stoker, stove repairer, plumber, or storekeeper, each of which occupations is now
separately distinguished in the occupation tables, without reference to the industry
which it serves, while in the industry tables all the important occupations serving
each industry are set out in detail by sex.
The principle underlying the construction of the occupational classification
used in the 1926 Census reports is that all occupations included under a heading
should form a homogeneous group. To achieve this it was necessary for certain
terms included under occupational headings to contain words indicating the material
worked in as well as the process, since the latter alone would cover m:tny entirely
dissimilar occupations. For example, moulders had to be separated into brick
moulders, candle moulders, foundry moulders, glass moulders, etc., each of
which more definite occupations appears under a separate occupational heading.
Certain other occupations, on the other hand, remain fundamentally similar,
whichever industry they may be connected with. For example, the occupation
of messenger in one establishment does not differ es3entially from that of messenger
in another, and all messengers are accordingly grouped together under one heading.
As a result of the differences between the 1911 and 1926 occupational classi-
fications there are but very few headings in the 1926list which can be compared with
those of earlier censuses. With certain minor exceptions the figures for professional
occupations are broadly comparable, as are those for certain others, such as farmers,
agricultural labourers, gardeners, game keepers and carmen, which were dealt
with on occupational lines in 1911, and a few occupations necessarily confined to
certain industries, such as tram drivers and conductors, railway engine drivers,
firemen and guards.


---

## Page 46

xxii.
The basis of the industrial classification is a simple one, namely, the product
made or the service rendered by the employer,. except in the ca_se of priv_ate d~m12sti~
servants where the industry is personal service and not the 1ndustry 1n which the
employer is engaged. In many headings _seyeral products have been grouped
together, either because the products are s1m1lar or because they are commonly
produced in the same factory.
The occupational and industrial classifications used in the 1926 Cen~us reports
were adapted to suit the requirements of this country from the English system
of classifications as published in the " Classification of Occupations, Census, 1921 "
and the " Classification of Industries, Census, 1921." The manner in which the
ists so adapted can be related to the parent English lists is detailed in Appendix D.
BIRTHPLACE AND NATIONALITY.
l. Persons stated to have been born in Ireland-the County name or tt Irish
Free State" not being inserted-are assumed to have been born in the Irish Free
State. Where nationality is given but birthplace is not, it has been assumed that
the person was born in the country of which he or she is a national. All persons of
British nationality and unstated birthplace have been regarded as born in the
County of enumeration.
2. All persons born in the British Empire ,have been treated as of British
nationality. Foreign born women married to Britishers have been shown as of
their husband's nationality; but women born in the British Empire and married
to foreigners have been regarded as British.
RELIGIOUS PROFESSIONS.
The religious professions, other than Roman Catholic, Presbyterian, Church
of Ireland, and Methodist, have been classified under the headings set out at foot
of Table 27, separate mention being made of all professions containing 10 or more
adherents. The heading "Presbyterian" includes those who call themselves~-­
by this name without qualification, together with those returned as members of
the Established Church of Scotland. Other Presbyterian denominations are shown
at the foot of the table. Members of the Church of England and the Episcopal
Church of Scotland have not been included under Church of Ireland except where
stated. The latter group includes all persons returned as " Church " or
" Episcopalian."
ORPHANHOOD.
For the purposes of Orphanhood statistics, step-parents, foster parents, and
adoptive parents, so stated, have been treated in the same manner as natural parents.
Where the information as to one parent is stated as "not known," that parent has"'~
been assumed to be dead, and the case dealt with accordingly.
Where information
as to neither parent is given, the case has been placed in the uNo Statement"
group.
DEPENDENCY.
The Dependency question contained in the Census schedule required
information in respect of married men, widowe~s and widows, and for the purpose of
Dependency statistics, while step-parents were included foster parents and adoptive
parents were excluded on the ground that double returns (by both these and the
natural parents, if living), which tbe:re would be no means of detecting, would detract
from the accuracy of the figures. It is estimated that less than 0·2 per cent. of the
children under 16 are adoptive.
Information· was not required on the Census
schedule as to whether a co11dition of actual dependency existed, and the dependency
tables do not include certain children who were in fact dependents, i.e., illegitimate
children, adopted children, children orphaned by the death of both· parents, and
children, enumerated in this country, of married men, widowers and widows who
were abroad at the date of the Census.


---

## Page 47

xxiii.
FERTILITY ..
No express fertility question was included in the Census schedule, but the
dependency particulars collected provide the series of comparative fertility ratios
shown in Table 32. The probability of a child being born in a year has been cal-
culated by means of the ratio :-
Number of children under one year of age
Total number of married men (or married women)
as given by the dependency figures in Table 29.
Being thus derived from the
dependency figures the fertility table contains an element foreign to its subject
in that parents were asked to include step-children among their dependent children.
Evidence of this is seen in the fertility ratios shown in the table for parents of ages
outside the normal fertility period. This defect should not, however, materially
affect the ratios, except perhaps at the older ages.
INFIRMITIES.
I. DEAF AND DuMB, DUMB, BLIND.-The figures giv~n are in respect of
persons c;o returned, except where the answers indicate only p1.rtial infirmity as
" nearly blind."
2.
CRIPPLED.
In addition to those persons specifically described as
'' Crippled," the figures given include all cases in which it is clearly indicated that
the person
(a) has suffered a total permanent loss of the use of arm, hand, leg, or
foot;
(b) is permanently crippled in such a degree as to prevent the free pursuit
of most ordinary occupations.
The following cases, treated as "Crippled," indicate the variety of the terms
used in answering the question on the Census schedule:-
Arm-deformed, disabled; hand-deformed; hip joint disease; knee-
decayed, tubercular arthritis in; leg-disabled, diseased bone in,
infirm, short ; on crutch ; paralysis-infantile, nervous, of arms and
side, of legs and back, of spine; rheumatoid arthritis ; spinal caries;
unable to walk.
3.
MENTALLY AFFLICTED.
All cases returned, whatever the degree of
affliction, are included in the tables. The following terms were used in the
returns:-
Afflicted;
feeble minded;
imbecile;
melancholia;
mental debility;
simple; weak intellect.
4.
Persons suffering from more than one infirmity have been classified to
that which appears most likely to constitute the greater, or greatest, disability,
infirmities being graded for this purpose from greatest to least, thus:-
Mentally afflicted ; blind ; crippled ; deaf and dumb ; dumb.
CONVENTION AS TO DOTS ( . ) AND DASHES (-).
Throughout this volume dots (.) are used in tabular matter to indicate
nil (0), and dashes (-) are used to indicate that information cannot or is not
intended to be given.
CORRECTION OF CENSUS DATA.
Every effort has been made to eliminate, as far as practicable, errors and
inaccuracies from the Census data. The process of correction began with the
Enumerators, who received full instructions as to the action to be taken in cases
of error or omission; it was continued by a system of check applied at each stage
of the office work ; and was completed by an intensive scrutiny and inter-com-
parison of the tabulation sheets. This scrutiny and comparison revealed a certain
number of incongruities, which, when material, were investigated and corrected
in the light of the facts disclosed. Errors of a minor nature (i.e., such as could not
affect the statistical value of the Census Tables) were adjusted more or less ar-
bitrarily).


---

## Page 48

xxiv.
PART III.
STATISTICAL NOTES.
The definitions, etc., contained in Part II of this volume apply with equal
force to the contents of these notes and to the tables contained in Part IV. The
Table references in Arabic numerals relate to the tables in Part IV of this volume.
AREA.
·The area of Northern Ireland, exclusive of the larger rivers, lakes, and tide-
ways, is 5,237 square miles, or a little over 16 per cent. of the area of Ireland. . The
distribution of the land is approximately : crops and pasture, 7 4 per cent. ; woods
and plantations, 1 per cent. ; mountain, turf bog, and marsh, 20 per cent. ; and
buildings, roads, etc., 5 per cent.
POPULATION.
The total population enumerated numbered 1,256,561 persons, of which 608,088
were males and 648,473 were females.
In Table 2 are shown the populations
enumerated at each of the censuses from 1821 to 1926, together with the intercensal
increases and decreases. It will be seen that the 12 per cent. shrinkage of popu-
lation which occurred during the decennium 1841-1851 was followed by a more or
less steady decline up to 1891 at an average decennial rate of nearly 4 per cent.
The census of 1901, however, disclosed an increase, small it is true, but the first
to be recorded since 1841. A more substantial, but still small, increase was re-
corded in 1911, and in 1926, despite war losses, a further increase was recorded,
this increase, however, only amounting to 0.48 per cent. in fifteen years. The
following table analyses the intercensal variations in population since 1871 :-

(a) Including the deaths of non-civilians which occurred outside Northern Ireland. It has not been found possible
to estimate the number of these deaths at all closely, but from the War mortality lists of soldiers from Ireland,
which were compiled by the War Office from regimental records, it appears that for the whole of Northern Ireland
the. non-civilian deaths which occurred outside the country numbered about 10,000 during the last intercensal
penod, exclusive of the deaths of regular soldiers stationed outside the country at the date of the 19ll Census.
It will be seen that, except for the period 1891-1901, the average annual number
of both births and deaths registered has fallen steadily since 1871, the fall being
particularly marked in the last intercensal period of fifteen years. Nevertheless,
the average annual excess of births over deaths for this period is very little less
than that for the preceding period, and greater than that for either of the periods

    1881-1891 or 1891-1901.
.

    During the 55 years 1871-1926 the births exceeded the deaths by 437,493,
but the increase in population which might have been thereby expected was more
than offset by migration losses, with the result that the population in 1926 was
102,629 less than in 1871. The net loss from migration between Northern Ireland
and all other countries therefore amounts to 530,122, or nearly 10,000 a year on an
average, after excluding the number of deaths of solcliers overseas during the Great
War. During the last intercensal period the net loss from migration amounted
to 97,573, giving an average annual net loss of 6,505.


---

## Page 49

XXV.
Migration. Prior to 1922 emigration returns \vere furnished by the Police
to the Registrar General for Ireland, who from them prepared and published monthly
and annual emigration reports. During the years leading up to and including 1922
it became impracticable for the Police to secure complete enumeration of emigrants.
The collection of the returns by the Police was accordingly discontinued in 1923,
and no steps were taken by this Office to continue to publish, for Northern Ireland,
separate emigration reports on the lines of those previously published for Ireland.
Instead, the more complete figures of both emigration and immigration, furnished
by masters of vessels and published by the Board of Trade, London, \vere used as a
basis to frame the estimates for the years 1922 and 1923 of the net emigration from
Northern Ireland to countries outside Europe. These estimates were used in
calculating the vital statistics yearly rates included in the Registrar-General's
Annual Reports. By the year 1924 arrangements had been made with the Board
of Trade whereby they commenced to publish figures of migrants between Northern
Ireland and countries outside Europe, instead of the figures relating to Ireland as
a whole which were published previously. The figures in the follo·wing table are
taken from the reports published by the Board of Trade :-
-

* Estimated from figures published by the Board of Trade.
Records are not available to show the migratory movement during the last
intercensal period between Northern Ireland and other parts of the British Isles.
A broad indication of the extent of the movement that has taken place between
Northern Ireland and the Irish Free State can, however, be obtained from a con-
sideration of the birthplace statistics compiled from the returns of the last two
Censuses. In 1926 the persons born in Northern Ireland and enumerated in the Irish
Free State numbered 35,132, their age distribution being such that they might
reasonably be expected to experience an average annual death rate of about 18.0

    per 1,000. The corresponding number of persons in 1911 was 34,978, among whom
    the expected deaths during the period 1911-1926 would have been 8,307. The
survivors of the migrants from Northern Ireland to the Irish Free State during the

    last 15 years thus amount to 35,132-(34,978-8,307) or 8,461 persons. In round
figures it seems that these persons are the survivors of some 10,000 persons born
in Northern Ireland who took up residence in the Irish Free State during the past
15 years.
The age distribution of persons born in the Irish Free State but enumerated in
Northern Ireland has not been ascertained, but assuming that conditions obtained
similar to those found in the converse position in the Irish Free State then some 24,000
persons born in the Irish Free State took up residence in Northern Ireland during
the past 15 years.
The figures thus deduced, although resting on certain broad assumptions and
not taking .._ account of any secondary movement of migrants to a third country,
nevertheless indicate that the net movement between the Irish Free State and
Northern Ireland was in a northerly direction and not inconsiderable.
It is unfortunately not possible similarly to deduce figures representing the
movement between this country and Great Britain, as particulars of age distribu-
tion by country of birth are not available. Such particulars in this case are essential
to the calculations, since the age constitution of the Irish born population in Great
Britain is probably much older than that of the remaining population owing to the
fact that the relatively larger immigration of Irish born persons in earlier years has
left a high proportion of survivors at the higher ages.


---

## Page 50

xxvi.
lntercensal Estimates of Population. It has been the practice to use an
estimated population as at the middle of each year for the purpose of calculating
yearly rates of marriages, births, deaths, and deaths from certain diseases.
For
the preparation of such estimates it is necessary to have the population according
to the last census, and the numbers of births, deaths, and persons who 1noved out
of or into the country to take up permanent residence.
Statistics of the first three
are of course available, but the only migration statistics collected relate solely
to British migrants to and from non-European countries, the remaining migrants
mainly comprising those between this country and Great Britain and the Irish Free
State. If there is a balance inwards or outwards of the latter migrants the estimates
of population, which are based only on census populations, and figures of births
deaths, and recorded migration, will differ from the true population figures, and this
difference, being cumulative, may assume substantial proportions in the case of
estimates for the years remote from the last census.
Such a defect in intercensal
estimates of population can only be adjusted when the actual net movement into
or out of the country is disclosed at the succeeding census.
Fo;r example, the esti-
mated population for the year 1924 as used in the report published by this Office

    for that year was 1,279,000, and this figure was reduced to 1,258,000 when the 1926
Census population became available. This difference is probably due to the fact
that the estimated figur& was for a year so remote from the last census, and took no
account of emigration between Northern Ireland and other parts of the British Isles,
which during the period 1914-1921 was probably abnormal. It is not considered
advisable nor in fact does it seem necessary to attempt to assume an average net
movement to other parts of the British Isles for the purpose of preparing estimates
of population for the years following the 1926 census.
Should the next census
for the British Isles, however, disclose a steady and measurable movement of this
kind it may then be possible to take this movement into account in preparing
population estimates.
Since no record is kept of movement between counties, towns, etc., a movement,
which, in the case of Belfast at least, is calculated to offset materially the natural
increase which might be expected from the excess of births over deaths, no attempt
is made in this Office to frame intercensal estimates of their population .
. The following table shows the estimated population of Northern Ireland as at
the middle of each year from 1911 to 1926 :-

637,719 persons or just over 50 per cent. of the entire population of Northern
Ireland live in the county boroughs and urban districts which cover 63 square
miles.
The remaining population, comprising 618,842 persons, is spread over an
area of 5,174 square miles.
The distribution of the rural population is shown graphically on the Ordnance
Survey Map accompanying this report, each district electoral division being shaded
to indicate the density grade into which it falls.
The average density of the 616
rural district electoral divisions is 0.2 persons per acre, and of these divisions 354,
or 57 per cent., show a density of less than 0.2 persons per acre. The corresponding
percentages for such areas in each county are : Antrim, 52 ; Armagh, 36 ; Down,

    36 ; Fermanagh, 91 ; Londonderry, 66 ; and Tyrone, 70.
Populations of Counties and Aggregates of Urban and Rural Areas. It will
be seen from Table V that if Belfast County Borough is excluded, the only county
for which an increase in population is recorded is Down, which county contains the
two county districts (Bangor Urban District and Castlereagh Rural Districts)
whose populations show the greatest increases among all county districts since 1911.
The greatest decrease among the counties occurs in Armagh, where the 1926 popu-
lation is 8.5 per cent. less than that of 1911.

## Page 52

xxviii.
Of the urban and rural areas into which the country is divided for adminis-
trative purposes, the total population of the county boroughs and urban districts is
now slightly in excess of that in the rural districts, the former having increased by ·
7.4 per cent. since 1911, while the latter decreased by 5.8 per cent. It should,
however, be noted that the two county boroughs of Belfast and Londonderry account
for no less than 72 per cent. of the urban population.
Since, as will be seen from Table I, the natural increase in population arising
from excess of births over deaths in the intercensal period would have amounted
to some 98,000 for the whole country if no migration had taken place, the 44,000
increase in the population of urban areas as against the 38,000 decrease in the
population of rural areas suggests that the. emigration to places abroad from
urban areas was to a material extent offs·et by movement from the country to
the towns.
Population of County Boroughs, Urban Districts and Rural Districts. The net
increase in urban population of 43,810 persons comprises increases in Belfast of

    28,204 persons, in Londonderry of 4,379 persons, and in 21 urban districts of 12,122
persons, against which are set decreases amounting to 895 persons in the remaining
10 urban districts.
The net decrease in the population of the 32 rural districts comprises decreases

    amounting to 44,140 persons in 24 districts, and increases totalling 6,360 persons
in the remaining 8 districts.
