## Page 53

xxix.
Of the 23 urban areas wh1ch have gained in population, 9 show increases of
more than 10 per cent., the largest increase being 65.3 per cent. in Bangor Urban
District. The area of this district has been enlarged since 1911, but it is estimated
that the 1911 population of the added area amounted only to 275 persons.
The
greatest percentage decreases in urban districts are between 6 and 7 per cent., and
occur in the two least populous districts of this type, Tanderagee and Keady.
Of the 24 rural districts showing decreases in population, 6 have suffered a loss
of more than 10 per cent., the greatest percentage decrease (15.6 per cent.) being in
Tanderagee Rural District. Belfast and Castlereagh Rural Districts show increases
of 12.1 and 40.2 per cent. respectively. These increases, and indeed all increases
and decreases in districts within easy access of Belfast should be considered in
conjunction with the trend of the population of the County Borough itself, \vhen
reviewing the extent to which the activities of the City have gro\vn, as measured
by the increase in population since 1911.
The population figures for the wards of Belfast and Londonderry are given in
Table 3.
In Belfast the greatest percentage increases occurred in Falls and Victoria
Wards, and the only decrease recorded was for St. George's Ward. In Londonderry
increases are recorded for all five wards, the greatest percentage increase being in
East Ward.
Population in Poor Law Unions. Of the 27 Poor Law Unions 1 the populations

    of 6 increased and 21 decreased during the period 1911-1926.
The populations
of all the Unions are included in Table 4.
Population of Voting Age in Parliamentary Constituencies.. In general, the
qualifications entitling a person to be registered as a parliamentary elector are as
follows : a man is qualified if 21 years of age or up\vards, with the requisite residence
or business premises qualification : a woman, if 30 years of age or upwards, and if
(1) entitled to be registered as a local govenment elector in respect of the occupation
of land or premises (not being a dwelling-house) of a yearly value of not less than £5,
or of a dwelling-house; or (2) the vvife of a husband entitled to be so registered.
A person may thus be registered as a parliamentary elector in a constituency al-
though not resident therein.

XXX.
The figures of percentages of electors to population show marked peculiarities.
In particular, attention may be drawn to the number of females upon the register
expressed as a percentage of the female population aged 30 years and over in West
Belfast. Having regard to the fact, however, that the Representation of the People
Act (Northern Ireland), 1928 has now received the Royal Assent and a new register
is in course of preparation, giving all females aged 21 years and over the right to
vote, further comment is unncecessary.

While the total number of houses decreased by 1.7 per cent. during the period
1911-1926, the number of inhabited houses increased by 1.6 per cent., this being
due to a considerable reduction in the number of uninhabited houses, the 1926 figure
for which is the smallest recorded since 1871. To some small extent the low figure
for uninhabited houses may be due to the particular instructions given in 1926 to
enumerators to omit from their enumeration empty houses in such an advanced
state of decay that there was little likelihood that they would again be used for
habitation. In this matter the enumerators were required to use their own judg-
ment. It will be appreciated that the census enumeration could not take account
of the state of repair of houses actually inhabited, and these must include a certain
number that might not be considered· fit for habitation. From the recorded figures
it will be seen that the proportion of houses inhabited has increased from 90.7 per
cent. to 93.7 per cent., and the proportion in course of erection has increased from
0.2 per cent. to 0.5 per cent. These figures, taken in conjunction with the 1.6 per
cent. increase in the number of inhabited houses and the 0.5 per cent. increase in
population indicate the existence of a relatively greater demand for houses in 1926
than in 1911. The important question as to whether the rate of replacemen,t of
houses is adequate is one that cannot properly be considered Without krici"wledge
of the age and state of repair of existing houses, in addition to the number of
houses completed and in course of erection, as recorded at the date of the Census.
While the housing figures contained in this volume and the Census county
volumes do not comprise all the data necessary for a searching examination of the
question they will probably be found to provide sufficient material to form a
useful basis for such an investigation.
From Table IX it appears that the features of the housing figures for the whole
country are more or less common to those for each of the counties, excepting for
the increase in the number of houses in Belfast County Borough. In county
boroughs and urban districts there were 5.6 per cent. more houses in 1926 than in
1911 as against an increase of 7.4 per cent. in the population. It is therefore not
surprising to find that in 1926 the proportion of uninhabited houses fell to less than
half that in 1911, while the number of houses building in 1926 was double


---

## Page 55

xxxi.
the number in 1911. In rural districts the total number of houses showed a 7.2
per cent. decrease, against a 5.8 per cent. decrease in population and the proportion
uninhabited fell from 10.6 per cent. to 8.2 per cent. Figures relating to houses in
county districts, ·wards of county boroughs and poor law unions, are given in
Tables 3 and 4.

(a) The figures given relate to each county as constituted at the date of the 1926 Census.
Private Houses. Tables 5 and 6 deal with private houses and the families
occupying them.
Of the 286,842 houses, of all kinds, completed and in course of
erection, 280,800 have been classified as private houses, and of these 262,940 are
in occupation of 273,668 private families.
The number of private families per 100
inhabited private houses is 104 for the Country as a \vhole, the rate being 109 for
Belfast County Borough, 125 for Londonderry County Borough, 102 for Fermanagh,
and 101 for each of the other counties.
93 per cent. of the private families in the
Country occupy a whole house each.
Of all the inhabited private houses in the Country 97 per cent. are occupied by
single families and 45 per cent. contain 4 or 5 rooms.
In rural districts only 33 per
cent. contain 4 or 5 rooms and 47 per cent. contain 1 to 3 rooms.
In 1926 the average number of persons per inhabited private house was 4.54.
Comparative figures for previous years are not available, but if all inhabited houses
are substituted for inhabited private houses and total population less inmates of
institutions for population in private houses, the resulting ratios, which appear in

    Table VIII, show a steady decline from 5.66 in 1851 to 4.63 in 1926. The rate for
1911 was 4.65.
Accommodation of Families and Persons. The average number of persons
per private family in 1926 was 4.36. Again reference must be made to Table VIII
for comparative figures.
The average number of persons per family, taking all
families into account, is 4.45 for 1926, which rate is the lowest of the series of steadily

    declining rates for the censuses from 1851 onwards. The rate for 1911 was 4.57.
The fact that the total number of families has increased, whereas the total
number of houses has decreased, since 1911 is worthy of note.
In this connection, a
comparison of the 1926 with the 1911 figures of accommodation of private families
in Belfast County Borough (vide Belfast County Volume, Table VI) reveals a decided
movement towards smaller sized units of occupation, 52 per cent. of the fam1lies


---

## Page 56

xxxii.
in 1926 occupying 3 or 4 rooms as against 2.9 per cent. in 1911. The :percentag~ of
persons in occupation of 3 or 4 roomed un1ts shows an even greater Increase, 1.e.,

    from 27 per cent. in 1911 to 54 per cent. in 1926. In Londonderry County Borough
a similar but less pronounced movement has taken place.
With regard to the size of census private families in rel~ti?n to units of oc~u­
pation in both Belfast and Londonderry County Boroughs, It Is found that whilst

    on an average the 1926 census family is smaller than that in 1901 or 1911 the average
size of families occupying one room has increased.
On the other hand the average
size of families occupying 5 or more rooms has become appreciably less than in
1901 and 1911, and is now smaller than that of families occupying 4 rooms.
The occupied room accommodation in Northern Ireland approximates to one
room per person, the variation from this average among the counties and county
boroughs ranging between 1.06 in County Dovvn and 0.86 in Londonderry County
Borough. Generally speaking, in urban d~stricts room accornmodation is above the
mean, and in rural districts below it.
Particulars of room accommodation in
county districts, wards of county boroughs, and poor lavv unions, appear in Tables
3 and 4, and a detailed analysis of the room accommodation, coupled with the size
of famHy, etc., in Table 7.
Of the total families in the country, 29 per cent. occupy
4 rooms each, the family of 3 occurs most frequently, and the most common com-
bination consists of families of 4, each family occupying 4 rooms.

Table X, in conjunction with Table 7, will enable comparisons to be made
between the density of occupation in each county. In these tables the density of
2 persons and less per room has not been sub-divided, but this is not intended to
imply any judgment as to what in fact constitutes adequate accommodation, since
this matter obviously could not be determined without a knowledge of other factors,
such as size of rooms, sanitary and water provision, etc.

of occupation js found in Londonderry County and County Borough, where 26.1

In the foregoing table a classification of private families according to the
number of rooms they occupy is shown side by side with a separate classification of
inhabited private houses according to the number of rooms which they contain.
It will be seen that while there are 99,716 families in the 1-3 room group there are
only 83,276 houses in that group.
In the remaining groups the numbers of houses
are greater than the numbers of families.
Apart from all questions of size of rooms, etc., a hypothetical re-arrangement
of families to bring their room accommodation into direct relation with that of the
houses available would therefore still leave 16,440 families of the 1-3 room
group in occupation of a part of a house only in each case.
Of all the private families in Northern Ireland, 7.2 per cent. are in occupation
of only apart ofa house. For Belfast County Borough the percentage is 15.1, and
for.,,J-.-eafionuetfy County Borough it is no less than 35.1.
The existence of many cases of shared houses may possibly suggest the need for
a greater degree of family privacy, which might be secured by the additional pro-
vision of either houses each containing a relatively small number of rooms, or
separate premises in larger structurally sub-divided buildings.
In considering the
type of house best suited for erection in the future, however, many factors need to
be ~al{en into consideration, and in this connection special attention is drawn to the
figures :relating to Northern Ireland in Table 7.
From these it will be seen that of
the 99,716families in the 1-3 room group, 14,306 comprise only one person each,
while, on the other hand, 12,918 families each comprise 7 or more persons. Corres-
ponding detailed figures relating to county boroughs and their wards, and county
districts, are shown in Table 8 of the County Volumes.
Tenements of One Room.
Of the 7,229 one room tenements 2,682 were enum-
erated in Belfa~t CQcunty Borough, 985 in Londonderry County Borough, 386 in the
urban districts, and 3)76 i11 the rural districts. In Belfast 3.1 per cent. of the
families are in occupation of one room each, the number of such single roomed tene-
n1ents having increased six-fold since 1911.
During the same period in Londonderry
County Borough the number of these tenements \vas nearly doubled, the proportion
of families thus housed being 11.4 per cent. in 1926. In rural districts the one room
tenements, which comprise a large number of one room cottages, are fewer than
in 1911.
Inhabited Buildings, etc., other than Private Houses. The population in each
of the types of inhabited buildings, etc., other than private houses, is shown in
Tables 8 and 9, together with the number of inmates of institutions. The institutions
are not individually identified as in 1911, but the number of institutions in each
category is given.


---

## Page 58

xxxiv.
The population of the 4,014 hotels and boarding and ~odging houses enumerated
numbered 30,423, \vhich is equivalent to about 1 in every ~0 of the to.tal popula-
tion. This proportion \vould of course vary greatly at different penods of the
year. The inmates of Poor La\V Institutions numbered 4,915 and of Prisons 354,

    as against 7,461 and 614, respectively, at the date of the 1911 Census.
Persons

    on Shipboard numbered 731 as against 1,021 in 1911.
17,661 persons were enumer-
ated in " Other Premises," \vhich include police and military barracks, residential
schools, premises occupied by caretakers, etc.
INSTITUTION INMATES.
Particulars of ages, sex, and marital condition of the inmates of Poor Law
Institutions, Lunatic Asylu_ms, Hospitals, and Nursing Homes and Prisons are
shown in Table 10. In each of these four classes of institutions the number of males
exceeds the number of females, in the case of prisons the proportion of male inmates
being 83 per cent. The largest number of inmates of Poor Law Institutions is

    found in the age group 60-64 years. In Lunatic Asylums the 50-54 years group is
the largest, \Vhile in both Hospitals and Nursing Homes and Prisons the largest
numbers appear under the age group 25-29 years.
Table 11 shows the former occupations of the inmates of Poor Law Institutions,
Lunatic Asylums and Prisons.
SEXES, AGES, AND MARITAL CONDITION.
Sex Proportions. Table 2 shows that at each Census since 1821 the number of
females has exceeded the number of males in the Country by at least 6 per cent.
and sometimes by as much as 10 per cent. The proportion of females per 1,000

    males has decreased, more or less steadily, from 1,102 in 1881 to 1,066 in 1926,
when the females numbered 40,385 more than the males.
Of the counties, Fermanagh shows the lo\vest proportion of females to males
at each census from 1871 onwards, the number of females being less than- the
number of males for each of the last four censuses.
On the other hand Belfast
County Borough has at all Censuses shown the highest proportion of females to

    males, this proportion being as high as 1,194 in 1881 and 1,123 in 1926.

The initial preponderance of males over females, due to the persistent excess
of male over female births, steadily diminishes until the age group 15-19 is reached.
In thi~ group the fen1ales preponderate for the first time. and the next three quin-
quennial age groups show rapid increases in the proportion of females.
The pro-
portion then diminishes over the ages 35 to 59, after which it again increases, this
time at a very rapid rate, until at the old ages there are nearly half as many more
females as males.

## Page 61

XXXV.
Ages.
Owing to the facilities provided by mechanical tabulation it was
possible to prepare tables showing the distribution of the population by individual
ages.
This distribution in respect of persons, males and females, for Northern
I~ela?-d, ~rban Aggregates and Rural Aggregates, is shown in Table 12, and a county
d1stnbutlon by single ages up to age 15 and by quinquennial groups of age
thereafter js contained ~n !~b~es 11 and 19 of the County Volumes.
A graph
showing the persons drstnbuhon for Northern Ireland bv individual ages
appears in diagr~m A .. In comn1on with all such graphs prepared from
Census enumerations, thrs graph shows that the ages given on the Census
returns are not free from error, in that substantial grouping occurs at certain
ages, particularly at ages
terminating with the digits 0 and 5.
The
breaks thus produced in the "curve" become neutralised to a large extent if
the records for successive individual ages are amalgamated into quinquennial or
other groups of age, and it is, therefore, usually preferable to base a comparative
study of the age distribution of populations upon a series of age groups rather than
upon individual ages.
So far as these errors in statement of age affect only the
ages immediately surrounding the age at which the grouping occurs they can be
almost entirely smoothed out by the use of a suitable method of graduation.
The age question contained in the Census Schedule required the age to be stated
in years and months. The object of including the column for months \Vas solely to
secure a greater concentration on the enquiry and so avoid loose or approximate
statements. While no doubt this object was partly achieved, it was found that an
appreciable number of people were unable to state their ages more precisely than in
years only. This uncertainty of exact age must have resulted in approximations
being given for the years in many cases, as is in fact obvious from the age dis-
tribution graph, and so long as such uncertainty exists regarding the ages of a
material proportion ·of the population any census age distribution is likely to
show heaped figures at the ''round number" ages, that is ages ending in 0 and 5,
and to a lesser extent the intermediate numbers 2 and 8. It is, however,
evident that the age statements obtained at the past four censuses have
improved considerably as regards accuracy, the graphical representation of the
population in quinquennial age groups showing progressively smoother curves at
each of these censuses.
If the numbers of persons at each year of age are plotted for urban and rural
aggregates it is seen that the "heaping" referred to in the preceding paragraph is
much more pronounced in the case of the rural than the urban curve. Whether
this is due to a differing general standard of education in the two types of areas, or
to n1ore frequent demands being made for particulars as to age in urban areas, is a
matter for conjecture, but it is significant that a similar graph of the 1921 population
of England and Wales (which was 79 per cent. urban as against the 51 per cent.
urban population of N orthem Ireland) shows heaped figures at corresponding
ages but these irregularities are relatively far less marked than those in the Northern
Ireland· graph.
One of the most outstanding features of the individual ages graph is the effect
of the low birth rates experienced in the years 1916 to 1919 and the high infant
mortality rates of the years 1917 to 1919 as shown in the numbers of survivors
of children born in those years, that is, children aged 7 to 10 in 1926. The deep de-
pression thus caused in the curve, the centre point of which occurs at age 8, must in
the ordinary course of events, recur at each succeeding census, but at correspond-
ingly later ages and with diminishing prominence, until the whole of the existing
population above the age of five has passed away. The effect of the high birth
rates in the years 1920 to 1923 and the low infant mortality rates in the last two
of these years is seen in the ''hump" in the curve at .ages 3 to 6. Another feature,
less marked, but nevertheless evident, is a depression in the trend of the curve over
the ages 30 to 45, probably due to the war losses and emigration. This depression
is perhaps more noticeable in the graphs of the population in quinquennial groups
of age which are shown in diagram B, the depression being greater in the case of
the males graph, o·wing, no doubt, to the war losses overseas, which have been
estimated to number approximately 10,000.
A graph of the population of England and Wales by quinquennial groups of
age, ~?t~mated as at the 30th June, 1926, and reduced to the basis of the total


---

## Page 62

xxxvi.
population of Northern Ireland in 1926, is also shown i_n diagr~m B .. Compa~ison
with the Northern Ireland graph suggests one or two 1nt<:restlng po1nts of differ-
ence. \Vhile the average ages of the England and Wales population (31.5 years)
is 0.9 years higher than that of the Norther~ Ireland population, the proportion of

    the former at the normal working ages of 15 to 65 years is 67.7 per cent. as against
only 62.9 per cent. in the case of. t~e la~ter: This su~gests that in t~e im~ediately
ensuing years, at any rate, the d1ff1cult1es I?- supporting the popul3:t1on w11l be the
greater in Northern Ireland. The depress1on at early adult ages 1s not n~arly. so
deep in the England and Wales curve, due no doubt to the lower emtgratlon
rates experienced in that country. At ages up to 20 years the curves show the
effect of the generally lower birth rates in England and Wales during the past two
decades, and the greater depression in the English curve at age group 5-9 illustrates
the remarkably low ebb to which the birth rates in that country fell in the years
1917-1919. Broadly the two curves show that the population of Northern Ireland
contains a higher proportion of both young and old persons and a correspondingly
lower proportion of adults under 60 years of age.
Diagram C comprises graphs which enable comparisons to be made
between the age distributions of the urban and rural populations of the
country. The depression centered at age group 5-9 is again evident in both graphs,
but its proportions are very much the greater in the urban graph. The abnormality
of the birth rates and infant mortality rates of the years 1916-19, which gave rise
to this depression, can perhaps be attributed directly to the Great War.
If this is
so, its effect appears to have been far greater in urban than in rural areas.
[See
also the effect shown in the graph for England and Wales (diagram B), where there
is a much higher proportion of urban population than ih Northern Ireland].
The average age of the rural population in Northern Ireland is 32.1 years and
of the urban population 29.3 years. The graphs illustrate this, and show that
the proportion of population is considerably greater in urban areas for all
age groups from 5 to 45 years, and consjderably less for all age groups thereafter.
Of the total urban population 18.0 per cent. are of ages 50 years and upwards,
while 24.3 per cent. of the rural population are of those ages.

    The total number of women of the child-bearing ages 15 to 44 years \Vas 292,922
in 1926, which is equivalent to 23.3 per cent. of the total population. These· figures
are slightly less than the corresponding figures for 1911 ·which were 297,205 and
23.8 per cent. respectively. The married \Vomen of the same ages, on the other
hand, have increased from 109,047 or 8.7 per cent. of the total population in 1911
to 114,799 or 9.1 per cent. of the total population in 1926. These changes are
referred to again in the Fertility section of these notes.
Comparison of Survivors and Enumerated by Ages.
With a view to testing the
accuracy of the age distribution of the enumerated population an estimate was
made of the survivors from the previous census population and subsequent births
at each year of age under 15 years and in groups of ages thereafter. The estimated

    survivors at ages 0 to 141ast birthday on 31st March, 1926, were calculated from the
births and deaths registered during the pre-vious 15 years, and adjusted for migratory
movement during that period.
This adjustment was arriYed at by taking the
recorded figures of net emigration for each of the fifteen years and rating them
up so as to total the net movement outwards during the intercensal period. These
rated up fig~res were then distributed by age in accordance with the recorded age
r-·--~S~~'ti&tics~of emigrants in the years 1911 to 1921.

Table XIV shows in comparison the survivors thus calculated and the .. il.umbers
enumerated at the Census. At ages under 3 years the survivors exceed the
enumerated by 3.3 per cent., this excess being balanced by the net
deficiency of the survivors over the next 12 years of life. The last two columns
of the table, which are shown in graphic form in diagram D, show the survivors
and enumerated as percentages of the corresponding births. At each of the first
three years of life the ratios for the enumerated population are appreciably lower
than those for the survivors. A similar feature was revealed in respect of the 1921
census population of England and Wales, and was considered to be probably due to
under-enumeration at ages 0 and I. At ages 4 to 14 the figures of enumerated
show marked preferences for the even years, which vvere not apparent in diagrari1-~--­
A owing to the masking effect of the varying annual number of births during the
intercensal period. It may be noted that for young ages the deaths are recorded
and tabulated in considerable detail as regards age, and migration is almost
negligible.
Since also there is reason to believe that the registration of births
and deaths is virtually complete, conditions for an accurate computation of the
survivors are most favourable in the case of young children.
On the whole therefore it would appear that numbers obtained by tracing the
survivors from the appropriate births are probably to be preferred to the numbers
enumerated at the youngest ages, and that the deficiency of the enumerated at
ages under 3 years is probably due to omissions of children of these ages from the
Census returns. In the absence of·a better explanation of these omissions, it can
only be sum1ised that in some cases householders regarded the Census returns
as an official roll, and as such not intended to include very young children.
At ages above 14 years the survivors from the population as enur:nerated at the
census of 1911 were calculated for groups of ages and compared with the population
enumerated in 1926. Agreement between these figures depends upon the recorded
numbers and age distributions of the 1911 and 1926 census populations, and of the
migrants in the intervening period being substantially accurate. The comparison
in question, however, disclosed considerable differences in several age groups,
particularly at the older ages, and this combined with the abnormally high pro-

    portion of persons enumerated at ages 65 to 74 in 1911 and the low proportion
    enumerated then at ages 50 to 64, suggests that the 1911 age distribution
was not sufficiently accurate to form a basis from which to calculate a standard
for testing the 1926 age distribution. Furthermore the total net emigration for the
intercensal period represented no less than 8.5 per cent. of the 1926 population and,
in making adjustments for this, recourse had to be made to many assumptions as
to ages, etc., which alone might detract considerably from the reliability of the
comparison attempted.

## Page 66

xxxix.
Marital Condition. Of the total population 62.7 per cent. were returned as
single, 31.0 per cent. as married, and 6.3 per cent. as \vido\ved or divorced. The
divorced and not re-1narried numbered 10 males and 5 females.
It may be noted
that divorce can only be obtained in Northern Ireland by the passing of an Act
of Parliament in each case.
In 1911 the single represented 65.0 per cent.,
the married 28.5
per cent., and the ·widowed 6.5 per cent. of the popu-

    lation! The sex proportions of the single in 1926 \Vere 1,012 females to 1,000
    males, of the married 1,032 wives to 1,000 husbands, and of the \\rido\ved 2,192
widows to 1,000 widowers.
In 1911 the proportion of females per 1,000 males

    in each of the conditions vvas: single, 1,025; married, 1,032; and vvidowed 2,170.
Both in 1911 and 1926 the number of married women exceeded thenumberofmarried
men by more than 3 per cent. This excess is probably in part accounted for by
the number of husbands abroad or at sea.

## Page 67

xl.
The distribution of the population according to marital condition and age has
changed considerably during the last intercensal period. The decline in the pro-
portion of single persons and the resultant increase in the proportion of married
persons can in part be attributed to the decrease in the nun1ber of persons at ages
under 15 years owing to the general decline in the yearly number of births which
has latterly been experienced. A further cause of the change in marital condition
proportion~ is the high average marriage rate among persons of 1narriageable age
experienced since 1911. The numbers of married persons of both sexes at all ages

    up to 64 years have increased, excepting in the case of males aged 35 to 44, this
exception probably being due to depletion by war deaths. It will be seen from
Table XVII that the percentage of persons married has considerably increased at
all ages from 20 to 64, the largest relative increases being at the younger ages.

NoTE.-The marriageable population has been regarded as the single, widowed and divorced aged 20 years and
over.
The changes in the marital condition proportions have effected changes also
in the sex proportions of the marriageable population. At ages 20-24 the 3.4 per
cent. excess of marriageable females in 1911 was converted into a 4.6 per cent.

    deficiency in 1926; at ages 25-34 and 45-54 the excess of marriageable females
appreciably decreased; and at ages 35-44 the decreased male population had .the
effect of increasing the excess of marriageable females. At ages 20 and over such

    excess in 1926 was 5,590 less than in 1911, thus reducing the percentage which it
bears to the total number of marriageable females at those ages from 20.3 to 18.4.
It should, perhaps, be pointed out that the comparisons made in Table XVII are
between men and women of similar ages, whereas husbands are more frequently
older than their wives (see Table 31), and this should be taken into account in a strict
examination of the sex and marital condition proportions.
EDUCATION.
When considering the figures in Table 13 the comments_ m~de with regard to
the mis-statement of ages, etc., in the sections of these notes headed ct Ages" and
((Comparisons of Survivors and Enumerated" should be borne in mind. The
mis-statements are probably not very serious over the range of school ages and tend
to balance each other. The apparent omission of children at ages 0 and 1 from the
Census returns, however, together with the vagaries of the age di~tribution amongst
children owing to variations in the birth rate as demonstrated by Diagram A, are
not without their significance in any attempt to forecast future school populations,
a problem with which it is not the purpose of these notes to deal.
The figures of persons attending schools contained in Table 13 have been
compared as far as possible, but necessarily in a broad manner, with such relevant
figures as could readily be furnished for the purpose or have been published by the
Ministry of Education.. In the case of public elementary schools the comparisons


---

## Page 68

xli.
on the whole showed substantial agreement. The census figures in total appeared
to be some 8,000 in excess of the figures of enrolments, 4,000 of this excess being at
ages outside the limits for compulsory school attendance. The excess of census
figures at age 13 amounted to 3,500, but this may possibly be accounted for to a
large extent by the fact that scholars are allowed to leave school at the end of the
term preceding their fourteenth birthday. The comparison of secondary school
figures also proved to be a close one, the number of pupils on the rolls on the lst

    December, 1925, being 8,460 and on the lst December, 1926, 9,012, as compared
with the Census enu!lleration of 8,569.
The comparison in respect of technical
schools, however, did not yield such satisfactory results. The number of students

    enrolled in the year 1925-26 was 22,456, \vhile the number returned as attending
technical schools on Census day was on}y 4,274.
The attendances of part time
students in this type of school invariably become less regular as the session pro-
gresses and as the Census day was veiy nearly at the end of the winter session for
evening classes, it is not surprising to find some falling off from the number of
enrolments. The Ministry of Education estimates the number of persons \vho were
actually attending technical schools in April, 1926, as approximately 13,000. It
would thus appear that the census figures for these persons were deficient by nearly
40 per cent. A possible explanation of this disparity may be that many part time
students who were normally engaged in following some occupation regarded the
technical instruction as of relatively minor importance, and omitted to record it
on the Census schedules.
It vvill be seen from Table 13 that 19.6 percent. of the total male population and
·17.9 per cent. of the total female population are attending educational establish-
ments. At the compulsory school ages, 6 to 13 inclusive, the percentages are

    97.0 and 96.7 for males and females respectively. At ages 15 to 19 inclusive, the
persons still attending various kinds of educationa:l establishments represent 12.9
per cent. of the males and 13.5 per cent. of the females.
The Public Elementary Schools provide education for 172,355 children at ages
6 to 13, or 96 per cent. of all children of those ages attending any kind of educational
· .... ,_.,~s.tp.blishment. ·
Of the children aged 3 to 13 years· not stated to be attending an educational

    establishment 100 males and 51 females -vvere follo\ving an occupation and 173 males
and 166 females were stated to be suffering from infirmities. From Table 33 it

    will be seen that 344 male and 266 female children under 14 years of age were
suffering from infirmities. By making deductions for the children aged 0-2 years,
and for those shown above as suffering from infirmities and not attending school,
it appears that of the children aged 3 to 13 attending educational establishments
some 150 males and 90 females suffered from infirmities.
OCCUPATIONS.
Proportions Occupied.
The occupied males and females as percentages of the
total population of both sexes have increased since 1911 in N orthem Ireland as a
whole and in each of the Counties, except in the case of males in Londonderry and
Tyrone and females in Tyrone, which show small decreases. In 1926, 45.4 per cent.
of the total population were stated to be occupied, 31.1 per cent. being males and
14.3 per cent. females. It should be noted that the "occupied" comprise all
persons whose normal occupations were stated and therefore includes both employed
and out of work persons.
While the percentage of males aged 12 years and over
who are occupied has generally changed but little since 1911, the percentage of
occupied females of those ages shows a substantial increase in each county except
Tyrone. For the whole country the percentages in 1926 were 84.8 for males and
35.7 for females.
In Belfast County Borough it was found that the percentages

    occupied at ages 12-19 and 65 and over were lower in 1926 than in 1911, while at the
intermediate ages of 20-64 the percentages have increased, especially in the case of
women.
The highest proportion of women occupied in Belfast·in 1926 was 77.1
per cent. of women _aged 20-24 years.

Numerically Important Occupations. When considering the grouping of
occupations into orders and sub-orders it should be remembered that such g_rouping
disregards the industries in ·which persons may be engaged.
For example, the
occupational order XXII (Transport \Vorkers) includes porters, messengers, carmen,
etc., irrespective of industry, but excludes clerks, carpenters, navvies, etc., em-
ployed in the transport service. The latter, together with others following the
same occupations in_ other service; are included in their own respective occupational
orders, i.e., XXVIII, XV, XVII, etc.
From Table XIX it will be seen that over 50 per cent. of the occupied males
appear under the main groups or orders II, Agricultural Occupations ; XXIII,
Commerce, Finance and Insurance; and VII, Metal Workers. The occupations
of most numerical importance among the males are: Farmers, Farmers' Relatives
assisting in the work of the farm; Agricultural Labourers; General Labourers ;
Proprietors of Dealing Businesses; and Salesman and Shop Assistants.
Of the occupied females 68 per cent. are included under the orders : XII,
Textile Workers ; XXVII, Personal Service; and XIII, Makers of Textile Goods
and Articles of Dress, the most important occupations numerically being Domestic
Servants ; Sewers and Stitchers ; Weavers ; and Clerks and Typists.
Table 14 shows by sex the number of persons included under each heading of
the full occupational classification. The distribution of the occupations throughout
the country according to the county or county borough of residence is provided by the
corresponding table (Table 13) of the County Voh,1mes.

* Only orders or sub-orders showing proportions of 5 or more per 1,000 occupied males or females are included, and the Single or Grouped
Occupations have been limited to those showing proportions of 10 or more per 1,000.
Agricultural Occupations. It will be seen from Tables XX and XXI that the
proportion of large agricultural holdings is very low both in Northern Ireland and
the Irish Free State, the proportion of agricultural lftbourers also being lovv in
consequence, particularly in the counties of Armagh, Ferrnanagh, and Tyrone,
where the farmers' relatives assisting in the work of the farm considerably out-
number the remaining agricultural labourers. It is clear that at the Census date
more than half of the farmers in the country employed no labourers other than
their own relatives. The high proportion of ''Others in Agricultur~l Occupations"
in England and Wales and Scotland is mainly due to the large number of gardeners)
nurserymen, etc., and their labourers; shepherds; farm bailiffs; and foresters
and their labourers in those countries.

* The Irish Free State figure is for 1917 and the remainder for 1926.
t The Northern Ireland and Irish Free State figures relate to 1926 and the remainder to 1921.

    In 1911, 84,173 persons were returned as farmers, 29,321 as farmers' relatives
assisting in the work of the farm and 30,132 as agricultural labourers.
In addition
42,988 persons were returned as general labourers, and the majority of these may
be assumed to have been agricultural labourers even though they were not definitely
so returned.
It thus appears that the farmers, their relatives, and agricultural

    labourers in 1911 numbered approximately 176,000 (if 75 per cent. of the general
labourers are inclu~ed as agricultural labourers) as against 145,889 in~1926;
The extent to which females participate in agricultural occupations differs
in the several countries of the British Isles.
In Northern Ireland the nurnber of
fen1ales engaged in such occupations i.s equivalent to 11.9 per cent. of the number
of males, while in the Irish Free State the percentage is 22.2.
In 1921 the corres-
ponding percentages in Great Britain were 7.1 for England and Wales and 14.3
for Scotland. In the Counties of Northern Ireland the percentages range from 10.9
per cent. in Fermanagh and Tyrone to 15.7 per cent. in Armagh.
Occupational Status. ·A consideration of the percentages in the following
table, which has been compiled from Table15, shows that 35.7 per cent. of the occu-
pied males in rural districts were returned as employers or workers on own account,
the proportion for urban areas being 11.6 per cent., and for the whole country 24.1

    per cent. The corresponding percentages for occupied females were 20.1, 6.9, and
11.5 respectively.

## Page 73

xlvi.
Table 17 shows for each occupational order and sub-order the distribution
of unemployed persons by sex and age.
Direct comparison between the Census "out of ·work" figures and those
recorded by the :Ministry of Labour in respect of persons registered for employment
is apt to prove misleading. The census figures which total 64,143 persons (46,668
males and 17,475 females) relate to all persons aged 14 and over, whatever their
normal occupation, who described themselves as "out of work" on the census
schedule.
Persons working short time and not actually engaged at the date of the
Census may or may not have regarded themselves as '' out of work," and, in the
case of females in particular, the fact that the alternative of u home duties " is
generally available may have influenced many not to regard themselves as "out of
work" even though they might accept paid employment if the offer became avail-
able, and indeed some may have registered at the Labour Exchange for this purpose.
The Ministry of Labour figures of persons seeking employment as recorded on

    the "Live Register " for the week ended 19th April, 1926, number 54,488 persons
(37,127 males and 17,361 females).
These figures include persons working short
time or temporarily stopped and casual workers.
Agricultural Workers, Domestic Servants, most Fishermen, and a few other
minor categories are not entitled to unemployment benefit.
In fact, very fe'\v of
these classes appear to be registered for employment, and for this reason the Ministry
of Labour figures for them are less than the census figures by some 4, 000 males and
1,500 females.
If this·adjustment is made to the Ministry of Labour figures the
census figures are still 5,000 in excess in the case of the males but are deficient by
about 1,500 in the case of the females.
The excess in the case of the males is probably due to the non-registration for
employment of persons outside the normal age or means hmits for unemployment
benefit; certain occupations such as agents, etc., not being normally insured for
unemployment ; and certain persons in receipt of sickness insurance benefit, and
on that account not actually working, being returned as out-of-'\vork on the Cerrsus
returns.
The deficiency of the census figures in the case of females is not accounted for
by any simple explanation. The absence of record as'' out of work" on the census
schedule of those engaged in "home duties" as suggested above may in part
account for the discrepancy, however. Short tjme workers not regarding them-
selves as out-of-'\vork may also have contributed to the deficiency.
The differences between the census figures of u out of work" and those upon
the "Live Register" for employment are perhaps not unreasonable having regard
to the different character and scope of the two sets of records.
The reasons sug-
gested, however, as possibly giving rise to the differences are by no means conclusive·
and a much more exhaustive examination would be necessary if the real facts of the
situation are to be ascertained.
National Health Insurance. The percentage of persons entitled to National
Health Insurance benefits shown in Table XXIV is, in the case of each occupational
status, considerably higher in urban than in rural areas. It is not possible to account
for these differences precisely, but there is little doubt that in the case of the em-
ployees the difference is partly due to the fact that only a small proportion of
farmers' relatives assisting in the work of the farm are insured under the National
Health Insurance Acts. Although many of these workers 1nay not be under a
definite '\vage contract they have all been regarded as employees for Census pur-
poses.
Of the total population aged 16 to 69 years, who are normally occupied, more
than one half are entitled to benefit.s, and of the total employees, apprentices, and ,,
out-of-work taken together in urban areas three out of every four are so entitlec.1,
1

Table 18 shows for each occupational order and sub-order the distributio~ of
persons who are entitled to National Health Insurance benefits by sex, and age.
The census figures relate to persons who ''when ill are entitled to benefits "
under National Health Insurance Acts.
The direct question as to whether a
person was or was not insured was avoided at the Census enquiry as it was con-
sidered to be too inquisitoria~ and liable to be regarded by some as an attempt to
detect individual cases of non-compliance with the relevant Acts.
From year to year the :Ministry of Labour prepare estimates of the number of
insured persons entitled to any kind of National Health Insurance benefit. A com-
parison of their estimate of the number of insured persons aged 16 to 70 years for
the year 1926 with the Census figures for persons of the same age is as follows:-

The Census figures in comparison with the Ministry of Labour estimate are
less by 6.9 per cent. of the persons, 4.6 per cent. of the males, and 10.3 per cent. of
the females.
In a considerable measure this difference probably arises from the
fact that> whereas the Ministry of Labour estimate includes a number of persons who
are not entitled to sickness or disablement benefit, the census question was possibly
answered in the negative by many people if they were not entitled to those par-
ticular benefits. The existence of such a difference must be taken into account
in any attempt to employ the age, occupational or place distribution of persons
'' entitled to benefit " as recorded in the census volumes.

has been prepared from the figures given in Table 16, and shows that among males

more than 60 per cent. of the workers are aged under 25 years in the groups : Ware-
housemen, Storekeepers, and Packers; Makers of Foods, Drinks, and Tobacco;
and Clerks and Draughtsrnen. Of the female Agricultural Workers 59 per cent.
are aged 4o years or over.

BIRTHPLACE AND NATIONALITY.
Summary of Birthplaces. Table 23 shows, by sex, the numbers of persons born

Only 0.6 per cent. were born outside the
British Isles, and two-fifths of these were born in the British Dominions and Colonies.
Persons born in foreign countries numbered 4,628, and 92 persons were born at sea.

ORPHANHOOD.
Table28shows that of the 388,886 children enumerated in the country at ages under
16, 41,737, or one in every nine, have lost one or both parents. This figure excludes
cases of orphaned children whose loss has been supplied by step-parents or adoptive
parents, and 1,469 cases in which no statement as to parents was secured.
Of the

    41,737 orphans, 58 per cent. are fatherless, and 35 per cent. are motherless, the
remaining 7 per cent. being orphaned by the death of both parents. It is inter-
esting to note that both the highest proportion of orphans who are fatherless (61
per cent.) and the lowest proportion who are motherless (32 per cent.) occur in Belfast
County Borough. The high proportion of fatherless to 1notherless orphans is in
part a corollary of the high proportion of widows to widowers, though to some extent
i:t may be due to a possible tendency to regard the fathers of illegitimate children
as dead if ·their whereabouts are unknown.
DEPENDENCY.
The distribution of dependent children (i.e., living children and step-children
under 16 years of age of married men, married '\Vomen, widowers, and widows),
according to the age of parent and size of family, is shown in Table 29, which also
includes particulars of the total number of children at each age in respect of parents
in quinquennial groups of age. It will be observed that the effect of the fluctuating
birth rates experienced during the period 1916-1923 (see "Ages" section of these
Notes) is again evidenced by the irregular distribution of the figures showing the
total nun'lber of children at each age.
In consequence the distribution of all the
dependency figures must be regarded as being to some extent abnormal.


---

## Page 79

Quality of Data.. With regard to the quality of the data, it was found, as is
commonly the case with such complex census enquiries, thatthedependencycolumns
on the schedules contained many errors, e.g. (1) the omission of the dependency
return altogether when from the face of the schedule there were children who should
have been included (2) the inclusion of grandchildren or children other than those
covered by the instructions, (3) the mis-statement of the ~ependents' .ages through
the placing of crosses in the wrong columns.
Whenever It was P?ss.Ible to do ~o,
corrections of obvious errors were freely made, and although omiSSions and mis-
statements not inconsistent with the rest of the information returned on the schedules
must have remained undetected, their extent can be measured broadly by the
comparison of dependency figures with other available data. The following table
compares, age by age, the'' dependent" children with the total children enumerated
in the country.

Compared with the total enumerated under the age. of 16, the total number
of children returned as ~'dependents" is less by 9.2 per cent. This does not neces-
sarily indicate that the returns were deficient to that extent, as a reference to the
form of the dependency enquiry will show. The question was only asked.of.married
inen, widows and widowers, and illegitimate children (whose mothers had not
subsequently married) were thus properly omitted from the return of dependents
although i~cluded among the total enumerated. Their numbers are not precisely
lmown, but from the births in the 16 years preceding the census they may be esti-
mated at nearly 4 per cent. of the total. The presence among the enumerated and
not among the dependents of children both of whose parents were dead (0.8 per cent.
of the total) and of some of the children in respect of whom orphanhood particulars
were not given (0.4 per cent. of the total) can be regarded as accounting for about
another 1 per cent. of the total difference of 9.2 per cent.
Further, children within
the country of parents outside the country would be included with the enumerated
but not the dependents, while children outside the country of parents enumerated
in the country would appear among the dependents but not the enumerated. It
may reasonably be assumed that in cases where one parent was outsidethe country
at Census date the children were more often with the mother, and the number of
children who were accordingly enumerated but not included in the dependency
return, can, therefore, be estimated from the excess of married women enumerated
over married men enumerated, i.e., 6,165. The children of this number of married
women might well account for another 2} to 3 per cent. of the total difference. It
thus appears that some 7 to 8 per cent. of the total difference between enumerated


---

## Page 80

liii.
and dependent children can be satisfactorily accounted for, and that the unexplained
portion of the difference, which may be attributed to omission of particulars from
the dependency columns of the schedule, probably represents between It to 2 per
cent. of the total children enumerated under 16 years of age.
It will be seen that the percentage excess of enumerated over dependent children
generally increases ·with the ages of the children, and it is therefore not unreasonable
to suppose that the portion of the difference due to the emission of dependency par-
ticulars is insignificantly small at the younger ages, but correspondingly larger at
ages approaching 15 years.
If the enumerated and dependents are compared for each of the three de-
pendency classes, i.e., married men, wido-ws,, and widowers, it is found that the
dependents of married men are only 6.6-per Gent. less than the enumerated children
whose parents were both alive, but in the other two classes the deficiencies are 20.3
per cent. and 25.2 per cent. i·espectively for widows and widowers. The higher
deficiencies of the wido·wed classes are perhaps to a large extent due to the more
frequent separation of child from parent among widowers and widows than among
married couples, and to the inclusion among the fatherless orphans of a relatively
large proportion of illegitimate children, the whereabouts of whose fathers are un-
known.
Of the total number of married women enumerated 91 per cent.\vere enumerated
on the same schedule as their husbands, and in these cases the husband's dependency
return was associated with his wife, thus providing the material for the fourth class
of parent, included in the Dependency tables, i.e., 1parried women enumerated
with their husbands.
Size of Dependent Families. The· sizes of the various dependent families,
enumerated as such, i.e., the numbers of children and step-children under 16 years
of age, varied from 0 to a maximum of 12 and were distributed as follows:-

For each class of parent, families of no dependent children-with which may
probably be included the majority of cases in which dependency particulars were
not stated on the census schedule-are far more numerous than those of any other
size; they comprise both childless families and families in which the youngest

    child is 16 years of age or more, and account for 35 to 40 per cent. of the total families
of married persons and some 80 per cent. of the total families of widowers
and widows.
Of the families in which dependent children were returned, families
of one child each are the most numerous for each class of parent, and the
numbers of families rapidly diminish as the size of family increases.


---

## Page 81

HV.
From Table XXXI it will be seen that the average number of children per family
steadily increases with the advancing age of the parent, reaching a maximum between

    the ages of 40 and 44 in the. case of males and 35 and 39 in the case of females, after
which the averages as regularly decline.

Occupations of Parents. The distribution of the dependent children of married
men and widowers according to the occupation of the parent and the size of the
family, together with the number of children at each age, is shown in Table 30.
Of the occupied males the married men engaged in mining and quarrying occupa-
tions show the highest average number of dependent children per family.
Census Ages of Husbands and Wives. The numbers of married couples
(enumerated together) at quinquennial groups of ages of husband and wife are shown
in Table 31. A consideration of the distribution of the figures in this table sho·ws
· that \Vhile the average age of all the husbands is higher than that of all the wives,
such male seniority does not obtain at all the ages of either husbands or wives.
For example, husbands under 25 are married to wives -vvhose average age is a little
older than their own, but in the case of husbands over that age their average age is
higher than that of their wives, this average seniority increasing steadily with the age
of the husbands until, at ages 85 and over, their wives are on an average more than
13 years their junior.


---

## Page 82

lv.
FERTILITY.
The fertility rates for married persons shown 1n Table 32 are based on
the ratio :--
-
Number of children under one year of age at date of census
Number of married men (or married women) at date of census
as given by the dependency figures in Table 29.
Comparing this ratio with the fertility ratio more commonly used, i.e.,
Number of legitimate children born in a year
Average n urn ber of married men (or rnalTied women) exposed to
risk during the year
it will be seen that the numerator of the former ratio is deficient by the number of
deaths among the children born in the preceding year, and the denominator is
deficient by approlci1nately one half of the previous year's deaths among the
parents, but is reinforced by the inclusion of new marriages. The deficiency in
the numerator is to some extent offset by the inclusion of step-children.
The total number of dependent children returned as under 1 year of age was
found to be nearly 14 per cent. less than the number of legitimate births during the
year preceding the date of the census.
Nearly one half of this deficiency can be
ascribed to the infant mortality ..referred to above, and the balance to other causes,
the chief of which is the omission of infants from the census return.
The fertility rates given in Table 32 are thus understated by possibly some 5
to 10 per cent., and they cannot therefore be regarded as absolute. Since, however,
the deficiency is probably distributed more or less evenly over the normal fertility
ages, the value of the rates as showing relative fertility is unlikely to have been
impaired to anY. great extent.
~the~~t~o sets of ratios given in Table 32, that in the columns headed rc A,"
which presumes that the parents in respect of whom particulars of families were
omitted from the census return in fact had no living children under 16 years of age,
in all probability more nearly represents the truth.
In the following table these
''A'' ratios are compared with corresponding fertility ratios for England and Wales
as obtained from the statistics of the 1921 Census.

## Page 83

lvi.
In Northern Ireland and England and Wales the highest rates are the same for
both married men and married wcmen, but they occur in the first quinquennial
age group (under 20) in England and Wales, whereas in Northern Ireland they
are not reached until the second age group (20-24). This difference may be due to
the fact that the England and Wales rates relate to the year 1920-21, when the birth
rate in that country was phenominally high. In general. the Northern Ireland rates
are appreciably the higher, and do not fall away so rapidly as those for England and
\Vales.
Birth Rates. Table XXXIII shows the average annual birth rates at the time

    of the 1901, 1911, and 1926 Censuses.
Since comparisons of crude birth rates for
different periods may prove misleading unless the sex and age distributions of the
total populations used in calculating the rates remain substantially unaltered, rates

    per 1,000 women aged 15 to 44 years (married, un1narried, and total), which to a
considerable extent take into account any variations in sex and age distribution of
the population, have been included in the table.
Of these rates, that representing

    the number of legitimate births per 1,000 married women aged 15 to 44 years
provides the best criterion for considering the trend of the birth rate since 1901,
when it was 283 per 1,000.
In 1911 this rate had fallen by 6.0 per cent. to 266 per
1,000, and during the last intercensal period a further decline of 13.9 per cent. in

    the rate took place. bringing it down to 229 per 1,000 in 1926.
The illegitimate birth rate has risen from 5.8 per 1,000 unmarried women aged

    15 to 44 years in 1901 to 6.8 per 1,000 in 1911 and 1926.
This rate of 6.8 per 1,000

    per annum amourits to 204 per 1,000 over the 30 years of age from 15 to 45, which
is equivalent to one illegiti1nate birth to every five unmarried women throughout
the normal childbearing ages.

## Page 84

I vii.
The age constitutions of the five classes of persons suffering from infirmities
differ considerably, the blind being the oldest and the dumb but not deaf the
youngest. The deaf and dumb show the highest proportion with normal occupations
stat~~· Among the 13,067 persons returned as suffering from any of the infirmities
specified there were 2,446 or 18.7 per cent. ·whose normal occupations \vere stated.
Deaf and Dumb, and Dumb Only.-The numbers of deaf and dumb and dumb
only are both substantially less than in 1911.
Blind. The number totally blind has increased by 52 per cent. since 1911.
A
partial check of the accuracy of the 1926 figure \vas obtained by comparing the
number enumerated in Belfast in 1926 with the result of a special house to house
census of blind persons, taken in 1921 by the Belfast Corporation. The com-
parison was ren1arkably close and tended to confirm the accuracy of the 1926
Ce1_1sus figure.
·
Crippled. The crippled males were 53 per cent. more numerous than the crippled
females, a condition probably arising out of the \Var. Unfortunately, no comparable
figures for 1911 are available, and no possible means has been found to test the
reliability of the 1926 figures, which should be accepted with due reserve, both on
account of the broad interpretation, which necessarily had to be placed on the term
u crippled J) for classification purposes, and because of a possible reluctance on the
part of some crippled persons to disclose their infirmity by stating it on the census
schedule.
Mentally Afflicted. The mentally afflicted show a slight increase over the numbers
returned as lunatics, imbeciles, or idiots in 1911. Since, however, the 1926 return
was in respect of all persons mentally afflicted, whatever the degree of such affliction
and included persons described as feeble-minded, simple, weak intellect, etc.,
strictly comparable figures for 1911 and 1926, if they were obtainable, would
probably show a decrease.
Of. the 5,834 persons returned as mentally afflicted,

    4,163 or 71 per cent. were enumerated in mental asylums and 377 or 6 per cent. in
other institutions.
compiling the infirmity tables persons suffering from more than one of the
specified infirmities have been classified to that which appears most likely to
constitute the greater, or greatest, disability.
(See definition of "mentally
afflicted " in Part II of this Report).

(a) Portrush Urban District was extended on the 1st April, 1915, by the addition thereto of portions of the Rural Districts of Ballymoney and Coleraine. The portion of the latter
Rural District was at the same time transferred from County Londonderry to County Antrim. In consequence of these alterations the 1911 House figures shown for the County
Districts concerned arc partly estimated.
(b) Ballycastle Urban District was created on the 1st April, 1921, out of portion of Ballycastle Rural District.
(cl Aghalee Rural District was abolished and its area added to Lisbum Rural District on 1st April, 1920.
(d) Crossmaglen Rural District was abolished and its area added to Newry No. 2 Rural District on 1st April, 1921.

    • Exclusive of 139,416a. 3r. 9p. (Antrim 49,029a. lr. 39p.; Armagh 15,129a. 1r. 2p.; Belfast 1,723a. 1r. 8p.; Down 377a. li. 7p.; Fermanagh 37,165a. 1r. 15p.; Londonderry Co.
    8,255a. 2r. 18p.; Londonderry C.B. 380a. Or. 23p.; Tyrone 27,356a. lr. 17p.) under the larger rivers, lakes, and tideways; of 5a. 1r. 5p., the area of the 1fai.den's Islands
in the North Channel (Antrim} ; of 5a. 2r. 8p., land on shore of Newry River (Armagh) ; of 2,298a. 3r. 31p., land on shores of Loughs Erne, Ca<;tlehulme, and Macnean (Fermanagh);
and of the area covered by Carlingford and Strangford Loughs, figures for which are not available.

(a) Bangor Urban District was extended on 1st June, 1924, by the transfer thereto of a portion of Newtownards Rural District. In consequence of this alteration, the 1911 Population
and House figures shown for the County Districts concerned are partly estimated.
(b) Downpatrick, which in 1911 was a town under the Towns Improvement (I) Act, 1854, and formed part of Downpatrick Rural District, was created an Urban District on 1st April,
1925.
(c) A portion of Downpatrick Rural District was transferred to Newtownards Rural District on 1st April, 1924.
'
(d) Belleek Rural District was abolished and its area added to Irvinestown Rural District on 1st April, 1921.
(e) Clones No. 2 Rural District was abolished and its area added to Lisnaskea Rural District on 1st April, 1921.
(f) Portstewart Urban District was created on 1st April, 1916, out of portion of Coleraine Rural District.
(g) See note (a) on page 2.
(h) Trillick Rural District was abolished and its area added to Omagh Rural District on 1st April, 1920.

(a) A portion of Coleraine Union was transferred to Ballymoney Union on 1st April, 1915. In consequence the House figures shown for 1911 are partly estimated.
(b) A portion. of Downpatrick Union was transferred to Newtownards Union on 1st April, 1924.
(c) Two Dispensary Districts in County Cavan, formerly part of Enniskillen Union, are no longer included therein.
(d) That portion of the former Poor Law Union of Ballyshannon which is situate in Northern Ireland, was added to Irvinestown Union on 1st April, 1921.
(e) That portion of the former Poor Law Union of Clones which is situate in Northern Ireland, was added to Lisnaskea Union on 1st April, 1921.
(f) Three Dispensary Districts in County Donegal, formerly part of Londonderry Union, are no longer included therein.
(~) That portion of the former Poor Law Union of Castleblayney which is situate in Northern Ireland, was added to Newry Union on 1st April, 1921.
(k) Two Dispensary Districts in County Donegal, formerly part of Strabane Union, are no longer included therein.

APPENDIX A.
CENSUS ACT (NORTHERN IRELAND), 1925.

    15 and 16, George 5, Chapter 21.
An Act for taking the Census for Northern Ireland in the year nineteen hundred and twenty-six, and
for otherwise collecting statistical information.
(lOth November, 1925.)
B
E it enacted by the King's most Excellent :Majesty, and the Senate and the House of Commons of
Northern Ireland in this present Parliament assembled, and by the authority of the same as
follows:-
·
1. A census for Northern Ireland shall be taken in the year nineteen hundred and twenty-six
in the manner hereinafter directed and the census day shall be Sunday the eighteenth day of April in
that year, or such other day, being Sunday, as may be fixed by the Governor of Northern Ireland in
Council.
2.-(I) The Ministry of Finance shall superintend the taking of the census.
(2) The officer to \Vhom there are assigned the duties of Registrar General of Births, Deaths and
:Marriages for Northern Ireland (in thi3 Act called the Registrar General) shall make such arrangements
and prepare and issue such forms and instructions as he deems necessary for the taking of the census.
(3) The expenses incurred, with the approval of the Ministry of Finance, for the purposes of this
Act, shall be paid out of moneys provided by Parliament.
3.-(I) For the purpose of the census there shall be enumeration districts, and an enumerator
shall be appointed for each enumeration district.
(2) Such officers and men of the Royal Ulster Constabulary as the Ministry of Finance, with
the consent of the lYlinistry of Home Affairs, may direct, and such other persons as the Ministry of
Finance may appoint, shall act as and be enumerators for the purposes of this Act or superintend or
assist in the enumeration.
4.-(1) Forms for making returns for the purpose of the census shall be prepared by the Registrar
General.
(2) The returns for the purpose of the census shall be made with respect to the classes of persons
specified in the first column of the First Schedule to this Act by the person specified opposite each such
class in the second column of that schedule; provided that a separate return may be made in such
cases and subject to such conditions as may be prescribed, and any person claiming to make such a
return shall be deemed to be the person by whom the return is to be made with :respect to himself.
(3) The returns for the purpose of the census shall state the particulars specified in the Second
Schedule to this Act.
(4) Every enumerator shall, in the course of the week ending on the Saturday next before the
Census day, deliver to each person in his enumeration district by whom a :return is to be made, or to a
person acting on behalf of such person, a form of return; and on every such form there shall be plainly
expressed that it is to be filled up by the person to or for whom it is delivered, and that it is to be ready
for delivery to the enumerator on the Monday next following.
(5) Eve;y person by whom a return is to be made shall fill up, or cause to be filled up, the form of
return in accordance with the provisions of this Act to the best of his knowledge and belief, and shall
sign his name thereto, and shall deliver the form so filled up and signed to the enumerator when re-
quired to do so.
(6) Every person with respect to whom a return is to be made shall give to the person by whom
the return is to be made such information as he may reasonably require for the purpose of enabling
him to make the return, and every person shall give to the enumerator all such information as he may
reasonably require for the purpose of his duties under this Act.
5.-(l) Every enumerator shall, on the Monday following the census day and such one or more
next consecutive days as the Registrar General may fix, collect all forms of :return delivered in his
district, and shall complete such of the forms as on delivery thereof appear to him to be defective and
correct such as he finds erroneous, and may make all such inquiries as are reasonably necessary for
that purpose.
(2) Every enumerator shall also furnish on such forms as may be prescribed an account of the
houses and other buildings in his district and the population of such houses and buildings, and for this
purpose the enumerator may make all such inquiries as are reasonably necessary and every person,
so far as his knowledge extends, shall give to the enumerator the information so required.


---

## Page 158

6.-(1) The Registrar General shall prepare abstracts of and reports on the census returns, and
each such report shall be printed and laid before both Houses of Parliament at as earlv a date as may
be found practicable.
~
(2) The Registrar General may, at the request and cost of any local authority or any person,
cause abstracts to be prepared containing statistical information which can be derived from the census
returns but is not supplied by the census abstracts or reports and ·which, in his opinion, the authority
or person may reasonably require.
(3) A certificate purporting to be signed by the Registrar General shall be admitted in any court
o~ law as evidence of the population at the census taken under this Act of any county, borough, town,
district or other area to which it refers, and the Registrar General shall be bound, if possible, to deliver
such certificate to any person on payment of a fee of ten shillings.
7. Instructions issued under this Act may prescribe, among other things-
(a)
the mode in which enumeration districts are to be formed; and
(b)
the duties of superintendents, enumerators, and other persons employed under this Act;
and
(c)
anything authorised by this Act to be prescribed.
8. Every enumerator shall make a statutory declaration to the effect that he has properly and
sufficiently performed the duties imposed on him by this Act and any instructions issued thereunder,
and that to the best of his knowledge the returns and accounts furnished by him are correct so far as
may be kno\vn.
9. Information obtained for the purpose of the census shall not be used otherwise than in accord-
ance with this Act; and no person having the custody, whether by himself or on behalf of any other
person, of any forms of return, enumeration books, or other confidential documents relating to the
census shall permit any other person without lawful authority to have access thereto.
1 0.-(1) If any superintendent, enumerator, or other person employed under this Act makes wilful
default in the performance of any of his duties under this Act, or instructions issued thereunder, or
makes any wilfully false declaration, he shall for each offence be liable on conviction under the Summary
Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts to a fine not exceeding ten pounds.
(2) If any person by whom a return is to be made
(a)
refuses, or without lawful excuse neglects, to fill up or cause to be filled up the form of return
to the best of his knowledge and belief, or to sign and deliver it as by this Act required ; or
(b)
wilfully makes, signs, or delivers, or causes to be made, signed, or delivered, any false return
of any matter specified in the form; or
(c)
refuses to answer, or wilfully gives a false answer to, any question necessary for obtaining
the information required to be obtained under this Act; or
(d)
uses, publishes, or communicates to any person otherwise than in accordance with this
Act any information given to him for the pu112ose of making such return,
he shall for each offence be liable on conviction under the Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts to a
fine not exceeding ten pounds.
(3) If any person who is required under this Act to answer any question, refuses to answer or wil-
fully gives a false answer to that question, he shall, for each offence, be liable on conviction under the
Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts to a fine not exceeding ten pounds:
Provided that no person, being required to state his religious profession, shall be subject to any
penalty for refusing to do so.
(4) If any person employed in taking the census uses, publishes or communicates, without lawful
authority, any information acquired in the course of his employment, he shall be guilty of a mis-
demeanour and shall on conviction be liable to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years, or
to a fine, or to both such imprisonment and fine.
(5) If any person, possessed of any information which to his knowledge has been disclosed in
contravention of this Act, uses, publishes, or communicates such information, he shall, for each offence,
be liable on conviction under the Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts to a fine not exceeding ten
pounds.
11. The Registrar General, under the direction of the Ministry of Finance, may collect and
publish from time to time any available statistical information concerning Northern Ireland until the
next census is taken and published under the authority of Parliament.
12. This Act may be cited as the Census Act (Northern Ireland), 1925.

returns and
certificate of
population.

prescribed by

## Page 159

68
Section 4.
Section 4.
FIRST SCHEDULE.
First Column.
Persons with respect to whom returns are to be
be made.
1. Persons present at midnight on the census day in a
dwelling, lodgings or rooms, separately occupied by any private
household of which they are members, guests (including paying
guests or boarders), or employees.
2. Persons present at midnight on the census day on the
premises of any hotel, club, boarding-house, or common or
other lodging-house.
3. Persons present at midnight on the census day on the
premises of any public or private hospital, sanatorium, convales-
cent or nursing home, work-house, poor-house, infirmary, asylum,
religious or charitable community, residential school or college,
or residential institution of any other kind.
4. Persons belonging to the naval, military, or air forces
of the Crown and any other persons, present at midnight on the
census day on any vessel, or in any barracks, station, or other
premises under naval, military, or air force discipline.
5. Persons present at midnight on the census day in any ship,
boat, barge or other vessel in any inland waters or engaged in
any coast-wise or fishing voyage or lying moored or anchored in
any harbour in Northern Ireland.
6. Persons present at midnight on the census day on the
premises of any civil prison, lock-up, or other place of detention.
7. Persons who, not having been enumerated elsewhere
for the purpose of the census, arrive at any of the places or prem-
ises above-mentioned after midnight on the census day and before
the returns in respect of persons present on or in such premises
or places are required to be completed.
·
8. Persons not included among any of the classes of persons
above-mentioned.
Second Col·umn.
Persons by whom returns are to be
made.
1. The head, or person for the time
being acting as the head, of the household.
2. The manager, or other person for the
time being in charge of the premises.
3. The chief resident officer or other
person for the time being in charge of the
institution.
4. Such person as may be appointed
for the purpose in pursuance of arrangements
to be made by the Registrar-General with tht;
Admiralty, Army Council, and Air Council.
5. The captain, master, or other person
for the time being in charge, of the vessel.
6. The governor, or other person for the
time being in charge of the premises.
7. The person by whom the returns are
to be made with respect to persons present at
midnight on the census day on or in such
places or premises.
8. The person with respect to whom the
return is to be made.
SECOND SCHEDULE.
pARTICULARS TO BE STATED IN THE RETURNS.
1. Full name.
2. Relation to head of household.
3. ,Age in years and months.
4. Sex.
etc.)
5. In respect of persons aged 16 years and upwards, condition as to marriage, (e.g., single, married, widowed
6. In respect of children under 16 years of age, whether both parents alive, father dead, mother dead, or both
parents dead.
7. Place of birth, and
(a)
if born in Northern Ireland, the name of the county and town or townland; or,
(b)
if born elsewhere, the name of the country, or whether born at sea.
8. In respect of persons born in a foreign country, nationality, and whether resident in Northern Ireland or a
visitor.
9. Religion.
10. Whether receiving instruction at a public elementary school or other educational institution, and, if receiv-
ing instruction at an educational institution other than a public elementary school, whether in the daytime or in the
evening, and the name of such institution.
11. Profession, trade, or other occupation (e.g., farming, manufacture, service, etc.) stating precise branch and
whether an apprentice or learner, and whether still engaged therein or retired, and, if occupied in trade or manufacture
the particular kind of work done, of material worked in, and of article (if any) made or dealt in.
12. Whether ordinarily occupied as an employer, employee, or on his or her own account, and, if as an employee·
whether employed or unemployed, and the name of present or last employer, as the case may be, and such employer's
business.
13. Whether entitled, when ill, to .benefits under the National Health Insurance Acts.
14. Whether deaf and dumb, dumb only, blind, crippled, or mentally afflicted.
15. In respect of married men, widowers, and widows the number and ages of all living children and stepchildren
under 16 years of age,

2. If a house or part· of a house be let or sublet to separate households · or

a. In the case of Hotels. Boarding Houses, Clubs, Lodging Houltl and other

object to giving ·the information in this way he may fill in the particulars on n

Returns will be furnished upon claim being made prior to the census date on a
· form to be obtained from the enumerator, or from the nearest Police Station.
5. All particulars given in this Return will be regar<,Ied' as strictly confidential,
and· :will be use4 only to prepare general census summaries.
6. If any person whose duty it is to give information refuse~ to do so, or wilfully
gives fal~ information, or uses otherwise thafl for the purpose of making the
Return 'an'y information _given him for that purpose, he -will be liable on conviction
to a fine not exceeding TEN POUNDS.
. . ·
. _
:·
7. Thll Return must be readJ early on MONDAY, 19th AP,IL, in order
to avoid delay when called for by the appointed enumerator. Such questions as
may b:e necessary will be asked by the enumerator to enable him to complete
or correct this Retum. and to compile a :return relating to hollsing. If the head
of a. household, or the manager of an establtshmertt, be unable to deliver the Return.
personally to the.enumerator he may instruct another person to do so.
