APPENDIX C.
CENSUS OF NORTHERN IRELAND, 1926.
INSTRUCTIONS TO SUPERINTENDENTS AND ENUMERATORS.
Issued by the Registrar General pursua1zt to tlze Census Act (Northern Irelanil), I925 Section 2 (2),
with the concurrence of the firfinistry of Home Affairs and tlze Inspector-General.
N OTE.-Each Constabulary District is a Census District under the superintendence of the District
Inspector, or in his absence the Head Constable in charge.
Instructions as to the division of Census Districts into Enumeration Districts and the
appointment of Enumerators therefor have been issued separately.
Instructions as to the payment of travelling and subsistence expenses, the submission of
claims in connection with postage and carriage and accounting procedure generally, will be
issued by the Ministry of Home Affairs.
The following instructions deal with the duties of Superintendents and Enumerators
in connection with the actual taking of the Census.
I NSTRUGTIONS TO ENUMERATORS.
I. Personal Responsibility.-Each Enumerator appointed pursuant to Section 3 (2) of the Census
Act (N.I.) 1925, is personally responsible, under the direction and control of his Superintendent, for
the performance of the ·duties detailed below ; and special attention is drawn in this connection to
Sections IO (1) and (4) of the Act, a COJ.3Y of which forms part of this book.
2. Supplies.-Each Enumerator will be supplied by the Superintendent with the following:-
(i) This Book of Instructions.
(ii) An Enumeration Book, in which to enter and summarise particulars of housing and population.
(iii) A sufficient number of each of the different types of Census Schedule likely to be required in
his district (see paragraphs 10 and II below). When the district contains an Institution,
or Institutions, with a resident population of more than 50 persons, Schedules prepared for
delivery will be supplied.
(iv) Forms C.15: Claim to make separate Confidential Census Return.
(v) Form C.l6: for reporting to Superintendent when all Schedules have been collected.
(vi) A portfolio, in which to carry the Schedules during distribution and collection.
(vii) A cover, into which the Schedules for his district must be laced when the enumeration is com-
plete.
(viii) Dividing Sheets, for use when the Volume of Schedules is being made up (see para. 35).
(ix) Stationery, etc., as follows:-
1 Indelible PenciL
l Packet of Paper Fasteners.
2 Sheets of Brown Paper.
Twine.
3. Summary of Duties.-The Enumerator's duties fall into four parts: (a) Preparation; (b)
Distribution of Schedules; (c) Collection of Schedules; and (d) Duties after Enumeration.
PREPARATION.
4. Study of Instructions and Schedules.-The first duty of an Enumerator is to read carefully
the instructions in this Book, in the Enumeration Book, and on the Schedules, and to consult the Super-
intendent if any point is not quite clear. The Enumerator must also study all examples on the Schedules,
and acquaint himself with the manner in which the several columns should be filled up, in order that he
may be competent to answer any questions put to him by Householders, and to revise the Schedules when
collected.


---

## Page 163

70
5. Boundaries and Contents of Enumeration District.-The Enumerator is to make himself
thoroughly familiar with the boundaries of each part of the area for which he is responsible, as listed
in the Form (C.3 or C.4) annexed to his Enumeration Book ; he is also to acquaint himself with all
buildings, etc., within each such portion of his Enumeration District. In addition, attention must be
paid to those parts of the district which it will not be possible to specify in the Form-streets as yet
unnamed, stretches of tidal or other water, etc. Should the Enumerator have any difficulty as to
townland or other boundaries he is to refer to his Superintendent, and, if necessary, consult the Ord-
nance Survey maps in the latter's office.
6. Route to be taken.-The Enumerator will arrange a convenient route by which he can visit
every dwelling and enumerate every person within his district. If necessary he should make a pre-
liminary tour of his district for this purpose, or in order to secure the information required under para. 7.
In towns care should be taken not to overlook separately-occupied back premises or waste ground
used for camping.
7. Preparation of Schedules.-Before commencing the delivery of the Schedules the Enumerator
must, as far as possible, enter in the spaces provided on the back of each, the name of the County or
County Borough, County District, District Electoral Division or \Nard, etc. Approximate information
as to the number of occupiers in each townland or street will be needed for this purpose, and should be
acquired in making the preliminary survey referred to at 6 ; or obtained by reference to the Police Register
of Householders or to Directories. See also instructions for filling up Enumeration Book-paragraph
2 (a).
DISTRIBUTION OF SCHEDULES.
8. Time Limit.-The delivery of Schedules must be begun not earlier than the 12th April and com-
pleted not later than the 17th April.
9. Articles to be carried.-The Enumerator must carry with him when delivering Schedules:-
(i) This Book of Instructions.
(ii) His Enumeration Book.
(iii) A supply of Schedules of each kind likely to be required (including Schedules prepared for
Establishments housing 50 or more persons, if any), in the Portfolio provided.
(iv) A supply of Forms C.15 (Claim to make a separate Confidential Census Return).
(v) An Indelible Pencil.
(vi) A supply of Paper Fasteners.
10. Kinds of Schedule.-The different kinds of Schedule are:
Schedule Al. Sheet with 10 spaces, for use by itself or in combination with A2.
Schedule A2. Sheet with 20 spaces, for use as 'required in combination with Al.
Schedule Bl. Sheet with 10 spaces, for use by itself or in combination with B2.
Schedule B2. Sheet with 20 spaces, for use as required in combination with Bl.
The Enumerator's stock of A2, B1, and B2 Schedules will normally be very small, and care must there-
fore be taken to keep them distinct from the A1 stock, in order that they shall not be mislaid or issued
in error. To facilitate this, a dividing board is supplied with each portfolio.
If the Enumerator should
run short of any kind of Schedule before he has completed the delivery, he must at once obtain a further
supply from a fellow-Enumerator or his Superintendent.
11. Persons for whom Schedules are intended.-The persons for whom each kind of Schedule is
intended are as follows :-
-
PERSONS TO RECEIVE "A" SCHEDULES.
(i) The head of a family or household occupying the whole or part of a house or flat.
(ii) A separate lodger occupying a room or rooms in a house or flat and not boarding with a family
in the house.
(When two or more lodgers share a room or rooms they must be treated for
Census purposes as a "family.")
(iii) A resident caretaker of a dwelling-house; of an office, shop, or other business premises; or
of a public building.
(iv) An out-door servant (with or without family) occupying separately any building or part of a
building (such as a lodge, gardener's cottage, dwelling rooms over a stable, garage, etc.)
detached from the house to which it belongs. This clause does not apply to persons residing
within the precincts of an Institution.
(v) A resident proprietor, manager, or head of an hotel, club, boarding-house, common or other
lodging-house, hostel, or shop-employees' living-in establishment.
(vi) Any person falling to be enumerated as member of a private household, or inmate of an hotel,
boarding-house, etc., who claims to make a separate '' Confidential " Return.
PERSONS TO RECEIVE '' B " SCHEDULES.
(i) The chief resident officer or other person for the time being in charge of every public or private
Hospital, Sanatorium, Convalescent or Nursing Home, Workhouse, Infirmary, Asylum,
Religious or Charitable Community, Residential School or College, or Residential Institution
of any other kind.


---

## Page 164

71
(ii) The officer or other person for the time being in charge of any Vessel, Barracks, Station, or
other premises under Naval, Military, Air Force, or Police discipline.
(iii) The captain, master, or other person for the time being in charge of any ship, toat, barge, or
other vessel lying in any canal, river, or other water \Vithin the Enumeration District,
or as specially instructed by the Superintendent.
(iv) The Governor or other person for the time being in charge of any Civil Prison, Reformatory,
Lock-up, or other place of detention.
N.B.-All persons residing within the precincts of any Institution (e.g., families in married quarters,
gatekeepers, etc.) are to be included in the Schedule for that Institution.
The Enumerator will make such enquiries as rna y be necessary to ascertain to what individuals Schedules
should be delivered, the kind of Schedule to be delivered in each case, and the number of persons to be-
enumerated in the Schedule. Where the persons resident number more than 10, the Enumerator
should fasten to the Al or Bl Form one or more copies of the A2 or B2 Forms as necessary.
12. Entries on Schedules at Time of Delivery.-Before the delivery of a Schedule the Enumerator
will enter, in the spaces provided on the back and at the foot:-
(a) The name and address of the person for whom it is left, together with the name of the County
or County Borough, County District, District Electoral Division, etc., if not already in-
serted (see para. 7).
(b) The number of living-rooms occupied by the persons to be enumerated on the Schedule-asking
any questions necessary to ascertain the number.
N.B.-The kitchen should be counted ·as a room; but the Enumerator must not count a scullery
(i.e., a room used only for washing, cleaning, or cooking), pantry, cloakroom, landing,
lobby, closet, or bathroom; or any warehouse, office, or shop rooms. If any of the
living-rooms occupied by the family are shared with another family, the Enumerator
should enter the total number of rooms occupied, whether wholly or partly, and note
in brackets below the entry the number of shared rooms. Thus, accommodation con-
sisting of two rooms with use of kitchen \vould be recorded as "3 rooms (1 shared).
13. Entries in Enumeration Book.-As the delivery of Schedules is proceeded with, corres-
ponding entries must be made in the Enumeration Book, in accordance with the Instructions prefixed
thereto.
14. Claim to make a separate Confidential Census Return.-If the Enumerator on calling at
premises to deliver a Schedule, is applied to for, or served with, a Form of Claim to make a separate Census
Return (C.l5) he must, after ensuring that the Form is properly filled up, act as follows :-He will
detach the top part from the Claim Form, initial it in the space provided on the back, and pin it with
the initialled side upwards to a Schedule Al, which he will then deliver to the claimant. He will also
initial the second portion of the Claim Form, which he will then pin, with the initialled side upward,
to the Schedule to be delivered to the Householder in whose Return, but for the claim, the claimant
would have been included. He will then destroy the third portion, which is only for use when claim is
made at a Police Station (see instructions on the third portion of the Claim Form).
15. Schedule to be ready on 19th Aprii.-The Enumerator should warn all persons to whom
Schedules are delivered that the latter must be ready, duly filled in, on the morning of Monday, 19th
April.
16. Returns to be left at Police Stations.-It will be noted that the instructions on Schedule Bl
include a request that the Return made by any person in charge of a vessel may (if the vessel should
sail before the Enumerator calls) be handed in at the nearest Police Station. A similar request should
be made verbally by the Enumerator when delivering Schedules to dwellers in caravans and tents,
and to householders who are likely to remove before the Schedules are collected. The Superintendent
will arrange that all Schedules so handed in shall be at once transmitted to the Enumerator concerned.
17. Courteous manner essentiaf.-!)t is of the utmost importance that the manner of an Enumerator
should be courteous and conciliatory, both when delivering and collecting the Schedules. When de·
livering the Schedules it is the duty of the Enumerator to give any explanations that may be asked for.
COLLECTION OF SCHEDULES.
18. Time Limit.-The collection of Schedules must be begun on the morning of 19th April, and
completed, in County Boroughs and Urban Districts by the 24th April, in Rural Districts by the 28th
April.
19. Articles to be carried.-The Enumerator must carry with him when collecting Schedules:-
(a) This Instruction Book.
(b) His Enumeration Book.
(c) A small supply of Schedules in the Portfolio provided.
(d) An Indelible Pencil.
20. Reference to Enumeration Book.-In the course of the collection of the Schedules the Enumer-
ation Book is to be used in the manner specified in the Instructions prefixed thereto.


---

## Page 165

72
21. Order of Collection.-The Enumerator must complete on Monday, 19th April, the collection
of Schedules from all hotels, clubs, boarding and lodging-houses in his district; and also-if his djstrict
should include part of any harbour, tideway, river or canal-those from all vessels and boats therein.
The collection may thereafter proceed along the line of route fixed by the Enumerator at the outset,
care being taken to revisit any premises noted in his Enumeration Book as vacant or in course of
erection, in case they may have become occupied since his previous visit.
22. Treatment of Census Schedules in cases of Removals.-Cases will arise in which a family will
remove from one house to another between the times of delivery and collection of the Census Schedules
If removal is between houses both of which are within the Enumeration District no difficulty should
arise, and the family must be enumerated as resident in the house in which they spent the Census night.
Cases of removal out of or into the Enumeration District must be dealt with as follows :-
A.
Families who moved out of Enumerator's District after Schedules had been delivered to the,nz.
(a) Such of these families as did not spend Census night within the Enumerator's District are not
to be enumerated by him.
(b) The Enumerator is to use every endeavour to secure completed Schedules in respect of the
remainder of these families, i.e., those who spent Census night within his Enumeration
District. The Enumerator will :-
i. receive Schedules transmitted in accordance with the Superintendent's instructions ; or
ii. fill up a Schedule himself from information obtained from neighbours of the persons
who have removed.
(Should the original Schedule completed by the householder
come to the Enumerator's hands later, he is to destroy the provisional Schedule filled
up by himself.)
NoTE.-If a Schedule cannot be obtained or filled up by the Enumerator, because the
family have moved to a place outside Northern Ireland or for any other reason, he
is to insert a note to this effect in the Remarks Column of Table l of his Enumeration
Book.
B. Families who moved into the Enumerator's District after delivery had taken place in that District.
(a) Such of these families as did not spend Census night within the Enumerator's District are not
to be enumerated by him. In the event of Schedules for these families not having been
collected or delivered at a Police Station, the Enumerator is to collect them and hand them
to the officer in charge of his Police Station without delay.
(b) In respect of families who spent Census night in his Enumeration District, the Enumerator
is to obtain completed Schedules, amending as necessary the particulars thereon of
County or County Borough, County District, District Electoral Division, etc., which
were inserted by the Enumerator who delivered the Schedule.
23. Schedules lost or omitted to be supplied.-Should it prove that any occupier has not received
a Schedule, or that the Schedule delivered has been lost or mislaid, he must be supplied with one by the
Enumerator and requested to fill it up forthwith. If necessary, the Enumerator is to enter the par-
ticulars himself.
24.
Houseless Persons.-The Enumerator must endeavour to number any dwellers in caravans
and tents, or vagrants who spent the night in barns, sheds, or in the open air, in his district during the
Census night. Schedules are to be filled up for such persons with the best particulars the Enumerator
is able to obtain. The ordinary police patrols will assist in this task, and transmit to the Enumerator
(in accordance with arrangements to be made by the Superintendent) any Schedules relating to persons
interrogated in his district.
Should it be found that information as to any houseless family or persons
has been entered on more than one Schedule, the duplicate Schedule or entry must be cancelled.
25. Examination of Schedules.-On collecting a Schedule it is the duty of the Enumerator
thoroughly to examine it and to satisfy himself that it has been fully and carefully filled up and signed
by the Householder or other responsible person. Particular attention must be paid in this connection
to the points dealt with in the Notes appended to these Instructions. The proper scrutiny of Schedules
is a matter of great importance, as upon it the accuracy of the Census largely depends.
26. Incomplete or Inaccurate Returns.-If any entry is illegible or incomplete, or if the meaning
of any entry is not clear, or the particulars stated in one part of the form contradict those given in
another part, or if it is otherwise found that a mistake has been made in the entry, the Enumerator
must ask any questions necessary to enable him to complete or correct it. Inquiries as to Religion
are not to be pressed [see notes on Col. (h)], and those concerning infirmities should be made with all
possible delicacy.
If the Schedule collected omits particulars of any person who should have been included thereon,
but who has gone away from the house before full particulars were obtained, the sex and probable
age of such person should be ascertained and entered, together with such dther particulars as may be
available; and in the absence of the name of the person, the words ''Person Unknown" should be
inserted-in Column (a) of the Schedule.
When making corrections, a line must be drawn through the erroneous entry and the corrections
written legibly. No erasures should be made.
27. Wilfully false answers.-Should any instance occur in which, to the Enumerator's knowledge,
the information on the Schedule or in reply to questions has in any particular been given with the


---

## Page 166

73
intention to mislead, he should explain to the person who gave such information that he is making
himself liable to penalties under Section lO of the Ctnsus Act ; and if the infonnation is not then
corrected the Enumerator should make a detailed report of the circumstances to his Superintendent
without delay.
28.
Refusal to give information.-Any person hesitating to fill up the Schedule or to answer
questions (other than those concerning Religion) which the Enumerator may find it necessary to put
for the purpose of carrying out these Instructions, should be assured that no facts relating to individuals
will be disclosed, or the Returns utilised for any other purposes than that of compiling figures for use
in general summaries.
If, however, an Enumerator should meet with persistent refusal, he is to explain
to the person refusing to give the information that he is making himself liable to penalties under Section
10 of the Census Act, and, if the information is still refused, the Enumerator should make a detailed
report of the circumstances to his Superintendent without delay.
29.-In every c:~.se in which a report is made by the Enumerator to his Superintendent under
paragraphs 27 and 28, the Enumerator should fill up a fresh Schedule, giving the particulars which, so
far as his knowledge extends, would otherwise have been given by the person refusing or ans\vering
falsely. The inaccurate or incomplete Schedule given to him by the householder should be forwarded,
together with the Enumerator's report, to the Superintendent.
30.
Confidential nature of lnformation.-The Enumerator is reminded that the whole of the
information contained in the Schedules collected by hin1, and the whole of the information received by
him, orally in response to any enquiries, is strictly confidential. Any breach of the law on this point
(Section lO (4) of the Census Act) will render him liable to imprisonment. This prohibition applies
not only to personal but also fb any statistical or numerical information acquired in the course of
the Enumeration.
DUTIES AFTER ENUMERATION.
31. Report to Superintendent.-The Enumerator, having collected oraccoun ted for all the Schedules
in his district (see Instructions in Enumeration Book), must at once fill up, affix a half-penny postage
stamp, and despatch to his Superintendent Form C.l6, intimating that he has completed the Enumera-
tion, This should be posted in the case of Urban Districts and County Boroughs on the night of
Saturday, the 24th April, and in the case of Rural Districts on the night of \Vednesday, the 28th April.
If any Schedules are outstanding on the days respectively mentioned, the Enumerator must notify
his Superintendent forthwith and ask for instn1ctions.
32. Transcription of separate Confidential Census Returns.-Persons who have been allow·ed to
make a separate Confidential Census Return will be included in Columns (a) and (b) only of the Schedule
for the household to which they belong. The rest of the particulars in respect of each such person
must be copied by the Enumerator from the claimant's separate Return into the remaining columns
of the Householder's Schedule on the line on which the entries were begun by the Head. The House-
holder's Schedule will then be complete, and should contain the particulars in respect of all persons
falling to be included, whether they made a claim or not. The separate Confidential Census Schedules
are then to be laid aside and not included in any of the following operations.
33. Arrangement and Numbering of Schedules.-The Enumerator must now arrange his Schedules
so as to bring together those relating to each sub-division of his district, and number them consecutively
in the order in which they are entered in his Enumeration Book, giving a single series of nt:..mtcrs to

    the whole, thus : 1-50, To\vnland A; 51-114, To\\'Tlland B; 115-182, Town C, etc. (see paragraphs
1 and 2 of Instructions in the Enumeration Book). The Schedules relating to vagrants, if any, in each
sub-division of the district must be brought together, and the group in each sub-division given a single
number.
34.
Revision of Schedules.-The Schedules, having been arranged and numbered, the following
action is to be taken on each:-
(i) Carefully examine in order to ascertain whether any errors have been overlooked at the time
of collection. In making this examination particular attention should be paid to the
points mentioned in the notes appended hereto, and if further errors are found, enquiries,
where necessary, should at once be made and the errors corrected.
(ii) Copy the contents of any dirty or tom Return, or any Return which can only be read with
great difficulty, on to a blank Schedule, and substitute this for the original. The original
Return should be carefully laid aside and preserved.
(iii) Count the number of rral.es and females and enter the totals in the spaces provided at the foot
of the Schedule. Then count the n11111ber of persons entered in the Schedule and see that
the result agrees with the sum of the males and females.
Having completed the examination of each Schedule and cleared up all doubtful points, the Enumerator
must sign it in the space provided at the foot.
35.
Binding of Schedules.-The Schedules should now be laced into the cover provided for the
purpose, the sections relating to the various townlands, etc., comprising the district being placed in
the order shown in Table 2 of the Enumeration Book. The numbers, affixed in accordance with para.
33, must run from the front to the back of the volume, and Dividing Sheets are to be inserted: in
Rural Districts, between each townland, town, o:r part thereof ; in Urban Districts, and County
Boroughs, between each " Division " (see Table 2 of Enumeration Book).


---

## Page 167

74
36.
Statutory Decraration.-On the conclusion of all his duties, as set out in this and jn the
Enumeration Book, the Enumerator is to make and subscribe the Statutory Declaration required of
him by Section 8 of the Census Act. The form of Declaration is printed on the last page of the
Enumeration Book.
37. Transmission of Documents to Superintendent-Finally, the Enumerator must make up and
retain in safe custody for transmission to his Superintendent when required:-
In a parcel labelled No. I.
His Volume of Schedules.
His Enumeration Book, duly completed.
All separate Confidential Census Returns and originals of re-written Schedules.
In parcel labelled No. 2
His Portfolio, Instruction Book, and unused Schedules.
CENSUS OFFICE,
WHITEHALL BUILDINGS,
ANN STREET,
BELFAST.
IJ,farch, 1926.
L. A. BULL\VINKLE,
Registrar-General.
INSTRUCTIONS TO SUPERINTENDENTS.
I. Personal Responsibility.-A Superintendent appointed pursuant to Section 3 (2) of the Census
Act (Northern Ireland), 1925, is responsible for the proper conduct of all Census operations within his
district.
PREPARATION FOR ENUMERATION.
2.
Receipt of Supplies.-The Superintendent will receive for distribution sufficient quantities of
the books, forms, and other articles detailed in paragraph 2 of the Enumerators' Instructions in this
book. A set of the various documents for use by himself will also be sent, together with a supply of
brown paper and twine for parcelling material to be distributed to Enumerators, and a supply of box
cord and labels for making up packages to be forwarded to the Census Office.
3.
Checking of Supplies.-The Superintendent will check quantities received against the form of
invoice (C.ll) to be sent him, and notify the Census Office of any cliscrepancy or insufficiency.
4.
Enumeration Books.-The Enumeration Books referred to in paragarph 2 (ii) of the Enumera-
tors' Instructions are in two sizes, viz.: Sizes A and B, containing 54 pages and 66 pages respectively
of table 1. The larger sized book is for use only when indicated on Form C.3 or C.4.
In all other
cases the sm1ller book (size A) is to be used. All Enumeration Books contain 6 pages of table 2.
Super-
intendents will be supplied with the necessary number of books of each size, together with 25 loose
sheets of table l, and lO loose sheets of table 2.
Cases should be rare in which an EnUIJ;.lerator finds the
pages for table l in his book insufficient for his district, and the loose sheets of tables l and 2 should,
therefore, be retained by the Superintendent until he receives a special request for them from an
Enumerator.
Before issuing the Enumeration Books the Superintendent is to write on the cover of each the names
of County and Census District and the numbers of the Census District and the Enumeration District;
and then fasten securely within the cover the Form (C.3 or C.4), on which is listed the townlands or
streets in the district concerned.
5.
Forms of Claims to make separate Confidential Census Return.-C. 15.-In distributing these
forms to Enumerators, the Superintendent must arrange that a sufficient supply shall be available at
each Police Station in his district, for issue to members of the public who may apply therefor.
6.
Issues to Enumerators.-The necessary supplies are to be distributed to Enumerators during
the week commencing the 22nd March, 1926, the cheapest mode of transit (rail, bus, carrier, police car,
or hired vehicle), consistent with rapid and safe delivery, being employed. At the same time a sufficient
quantity of Forms C.l5 (claim to make a separate Census Return) and Schedules Al is to be supplied
to the officer in charge of each Police Station, who should be instructed to deal with such of these
claims as may be made at Police Stations in the manner detailed on the Claim Form.
7.
Individual Instruction of Enumerators.-When issuing supplies the Superintendent will direct
each Enumerator to study carefully the instructions as to his duty, set out in this Book and in the
Enumeration Book; and will, at a date prior to the commencement of the delivery of Schedules (Forms


---

## Page 168

75
A and B), interview each Enumerator, to ascertain that he clearly understands the procedure which
he is required to follow, and is thoroughly conversant with the situation and extent of his district.
The Superintendent must afford such explanations and guidance as are necessarv to secure this
result; and if, on perusing the set of documents referred to at paragraph 2 abo\~e, he should himself
be in doubt on any point, he is to submit the matter without delay to the Census Office.
The special attention of Enumerators must be called to those parts of their Instructions which are
printed in heavy type.
8. Houseless Persons.-As it appears improbable that Enumerators could effect, unaided, the
complete enumeration of persons who pass the Census Night in caravans, tents, barns, sheds, or in
the open air, the Superintendent is requested to direct the ordinary police patrols on duty in his district
during the 24 hours of Monday, the 19th April, to interrogate and (where this has not been done al-
ready) record particulars of all such persons. For this purpose each patrol should be provided \Vith a
small supply of Schedules Al.
9. Shipping Population.-The Superintendent is to settle, and make clear to all Enumerators
concerned, the exact limits of those districts which extend over tideways, lakes, canals, etc., so as to
avoid territorial gaps or overlapping. He will also make such arrangements as the circumstances
require to secure the proper enumeration of persons on board ships, barges, etc., requesting, where
necessary, the aid of Board of Trade, Customs and Excise, Harbour, or Consular Officials, in removing
any difficulty which may arise. Special arrangements, if required, are to be notified to the Enumerator
concerned for his guidance.
DUTIES AT TIME OF CENSUS.
IO.-Throughout the period allowed for delivery and collection of the Schedules, the Superintendent
will watch generally the progress of his Enumerators, and be available to deal at once with any case
of difficulty or emergency reported to him.
II. Schedules brought to Police stations.-The Superintendent is to arrange for the immediate
transmission to Enumerators concerned of-
(a) Schedules handed in at any Police Station in his Constabulary District, in compliance ·with
the request printed on Schedule BI or made verbally by the Enumerator;
(b) any Schedules as to houseless persons filled up by police patrols.
(See Enumerators' Instructions, paragraphs I6 and 24).
Schedules which are found on examination to belong to another Census District must be sent
to the Superintendent of that district, who will then forward them to the proper Enumerator, or, if
the other Census District cannot be ascertained, they should be sent to the Census Office. In the
latter event the Census Office will transmit the Schedules to the appropriate Superintendent.
·I2. Information refused or given with intent to mislead.-The Superintendent will, after investi-
gation, forward to the Census Office any Reports made to him under paragraphs 27 and/or 28 of the
Enumerators' Instructions, stating whether he considers that a prosecution would be advisable;
but in no circumstances is a prosecution under the Census Act to be instituted without the express
authority of the Ministry of Finance.
I3. Completion of Collection.-On the 26th April in the case of County Boroughs and Urban
Districts, and on the 29th April in the case of Rural Districts, the Superintendent should receive from
each Enumerator, on postcard C.I6, a Report, as to the completion of the collection of Schedules in his
district. Should the Enumerator report that he has been unable to collect all his Schedules, or fail
to transmit postcard C.l6, the Superintendent must immediately give his personal attention to the
matter, and take such steps as may be necessary to clear up the position.
DUTIES AFTER ENUMERATION.
I4. Receipt of Documents.-The Superintendent must arrange for the collection of the following
parcels from each Enumerator on or before 8th May, I926, by police car if practicable, or if not, by hired
car. If a hired car is used, a police constable must accompany it and ensure the safe collection of the
parcels.
In a
parcel.
labelled
No. I (
(a) All Schedules collected, completed, arranged, and bound by him in accordance
with Instructions.
(b) His Enumeration Book, duly completed and summarised.
(c) All separate Census Confidential Returns and improperly filled Schedules con-
taining information which has been incorporated with (a).
In a
(
1i~~~f~d
(d) His Instruction Book, Portfolio, and unused Schedules.
No.2.
I5. Checking of Schedules.-Having satisfied himself that the Schedules in each Volume are
properly arranged and numbered (see Enumerators' Instructions, para. 33). the Superintendent is to
make a general scrutiny of each Volume in order to satisfy himself that the manuscript is legible, the


---

## Page 169

76
inform1.tion complete, clear, and precise, and the figures at the foot of the Schedules correctly totalled.
If, in the course of this general scrutiny, errors are detected, steps must l:e at once taken to have them
rectified, and where any one volume shows a high proportion of errors or is otherwise unsatisfactory,
it must be examined in detail and returned to the Enumerator with an instruction to obtain any
deficient particulars and correct all errors.
No correction of substance in regard to the information given by the Head of a Household or In-
stitution should be made without reference to that person.
The Superintendent should understand that the exercise by him of energy and care in the scrutiny
of Schedules will save him considerable trouble, which would othenvise arise \Vhen the Schedules are
being examinated in the Census Office at a later date.
16.
Checking of Enumeration Books.-Having examined each Volume of Schedules, the Super-
intendent is to scrutinise the relative Enumeration Book for the purpose of satisfying himself that the
Instructio:1s prefixed thereto (especially paragraphs I, 2 and 15-18) have been properly carried out.
\:Yhen all inaccuracies found in the book have been corrected and omissions supplied, the Super-
intendent is to sign the Certificate on the last page.
17.
Confidential nature of Returns.-On no account must any of the information contained in the
Census Schedules or Enumeration Books or figures derived therefrom be communicated, either to
private individuals, to local officials, or to the Pre3s.
18. Transmission o? Documents.-.-\.s the scru.tiny of Schedules and Enumeration Books proceeds,
the Superintendent should fonvard to the Census Office, \Vhitehall Buildings, Ann Street, Belfast,
instalments of checked material, the relative Enumeration Book being strapped to each Volume of
Schedules, with any separate Confidential Returns or spoiled Returns. The instalment ("vhich must be
s~curely packed in one of the collapsible cartons originally used to convey supplies) is to be sent by
rail or other safe mode of transit, carriage paid, a letter advising its dispatch being at the same time
addressed to the Census Office. The final instalment and (in a separate package) all Census material
contained in the Enumerators' "No. 2" parcels, together with spare forms from Police Stations and
Police Patrols, should be despatched to the Census Office on or before the 7th June, 1926. In packing
the collapsible cartons great care must be taken to ensure that they are sufficiently 'full and tighly packed
to travel properly, and that they are securely roped and properly labelled.
19.
Superintendent's Report.-On the conclusion of all the operations the Superintendent is to
make a brief report to the· Registrar-General, setting forth any points encountered in the course of his
duties which may appear to be of interest, either in connection with the administration of the 1926
Census, or for the guidance of the authorities in connection with future Censuses.
20.-The attention of Superintendents is dra\vn to Section 10 of the Census Act, which is printed
as an Appendix to these Instructions.
CENSUS OFFICE,
WHITEHALL BUILDINGS,
ANN STREET,
BELFAST.
March, 1926.
L. A. BULL\VINKLE,
Registrar-General.
NOTES ON FILLING UP THE SCHEDULE.
Under the heading of each Column on the Schedule are printed Instructions, which, if read carefully,
will prevent any misunderstanding of the meaning of the questions to be answered. Examples of the
manner in which the Schedules should be filled up are also given ; and further guidance is provided
in the folliwing notes :-
Column (a).-The Schedule must include all persons alive and present in the dwelling at midnight
on Sunday, 18th April, together with such night-workers and travellers not enumerated elsewhere
as m::ty return to or an-ive at the dwelling next morning. A person who died after the Schedule was
left ~t the house, b~t before the actual moment to which the Census relates (viz., midnight of 18th-19th
Apnl), should be excluded, as should also a child born after that time and before the Schedule is col-
lected.
The names are to be entered in their natural order-i.e., the surname last.
Column (c).-Age.-The entry on the left-hand side of the Column should show th~ number of
C?mplete years of age; and that on the right-hand side, the number of completed months from the
birthday to the Census day. Thus, a person in his 26th year whose birthday is the 1st July, would
appear as aged 25 years 9 months.
'
The age of a person whose year of birth is kno·wn, but whose birthday is not known, is to be ascer-
tained by deducting the year of birth from 1925-g.g., a person born on some unknown date in 1857
would be entered as 68 years old.


---

## Page 170

77
For children under one year old l( 0 " should be inserted in the left-hand portion of the Column,
and the age in completed months given in the right-hand portion.
It is important to nnte that the present Census Schedules wm not be used for purposes of proof of
age in connection with claims to Old Age Pensions.
Column (d). Sex.-ln all cases care must be taken that the sex in Column (d) is correctly stated in
reference to the Christian name in Column (a).
Column (e).
Marriage or Orphanhood.-This column provides for one set of statements concerning
persons aged 16 years or more and for another set concerning persons up to that age, so that there
should be an entry of one kind or another for every person on the Schedule.
As regards Orphanhood, care must be taken to see that a complete statement is made. The '\Vord
"Both," by itself, would often leave it quite uncertain whether both parents '\Vere alive or l::oth dead;
and even where the particulars for both parents appear on the same Schedule as those for their children,
the words '' Both alive " should be inserted. \Vhen no information as to either or both of the parents
is available the entry should be, as the case requires, "Father, n.k.,"." Mother, n.k.," or Both, n.k."
(n.k.=no lmowledge).
An ex=sting Step-father or Step-mi)ther should be regarded as taking the place of the natural
parent; and where it is evident that the Schedule entry conflicts '\Vith this instruction (t.e., when the
words " Father dead" or " Both dead" appear in respect of a child described in Column (b) as Step-
son or Step-daughter) the entry should be amended.
Column (g).
Nationality.-This column is to be left blank for all persons born in any part of the
British Isles or of the British Colonies and Dominions Oversea.
It should be noted that:-
(i) A married woman is deemed to be a subject of the State of which her husband is for the time
being a subject.
(ii) A British subject who becomes an alien and is readmitted to British nationality, becomes a
British subject and not a naturalised one.
Column (h).
Religion.-No penalty attaches to a refusal to answer this question: and if the
Column has not been filled and the Householder declines to make an entry, the Enumerator is to accept
his decision without comment and write in the blank column " Information refused."
An effort should, however, be made to induce any Householder who enters the vague term u Pro-
testant " to state the precise name of the religious body to which_ he belongs. If the Householder should
decline to do this,. the Enumerator must note opposite the entry "Further information declined."
Column (j). Schooi.-Where a child is receiving instruction at a Public Elementary School the
letters "P.E.S." only should be written; but in all other cases the name of the School must be given,
followed by the word "Day" or "Evening" as the case may be.
Columns (k) and (1). Occupation and Employer's Business.-lt is most important that the answers
entered in these columns should be clear and precise. Detailed instructions as to the mode of answering
the questions appear on the back of the Schedule, and these must be carefully studied and used for refer-
ence in deciding doubtful points.
Column (n).
Infirmities.-The persons in respect of whom this Column should be filled up are :-
(i) Those totally deaf and dumb, dumb only, or totally blind.
(ii) Those who are crippled by the loss of one or more arms or legs.
(iii) Lunatics, Idiots, and those otherwise mentally deficient.
It is expected that the local knowledge of Enumerators will enable them to ensure that all persons
comprised in the last mentioned class are so shown on the Return.
Columns (o) and (p). Living children and step-children under 16 years of age.-Points to which
attention may be directed in filling up these Columns are:-
(i) That on the same line as the name of each married man, widower, or widow enumerated on
the Schedule are to be given the ages of all living children and step-children under 16 (if
any) of that individual, by the insertion of crosses in the appropriate age-spaces. This
applies whether the children in question are enumerated on the Schedule or not (children
living outside Northern Ireland should be included). No mark is necessary in these Columns
opposite the name of any child.
(ii) That in the case of two children or step-children of the same age last birthday, two crosses
are to be placed in the same column. The number of crosses will thus be equal to the
number of living children.
The Columns should not be filled up in respect of a married woman, even though acting as head
of the family in the absence of her husband.

APPENDIX D.
OCCUPATIONAL AND INDUSTRIAL CLASSIFICATIONS.
Occupations.-The classification used in the I926 Census reports is based upon the English system
of classification as published in the "Classification of Occupations, Census 1921." It \Vill be seen
that there are thirty-two orders, each covering the same occupational ground as the England and \Vales
orders. Eleven of the orders are divided into thirty-two sub-orders, each of which is comparable with
the corresponding England and \Vales sub-order, with the following exceptions:-

Industries.-The classification used in the 1926 Census reports is based upon the English system of
classification as published in the tl Classification of Industries, Census, 1921." It will be seen that there

## Page 177

iii
PREFACE.
This volume has been prepared in accordance with the provts1ons of
Section 6 (1) of the Census Act (Northern Ireland), 1925.
The Census

    to which it relates was taken as at midnight of the 18th-19th April, 1926.
The Census publications consist of :-
1.
SEVEN CouNTY VoLUMES, including a volume relating to BELFAST CoUNTY
BoROUGH.
These volumes contain population and housing figures for
administrative areas and townlands; and tables dealing with age,
marital condition, education, occupations, industries, birthplaces,
religious professions, orphanhood, and infirmities ; together with
statistical notes on some of the more outstanding features disclosed.
2.
A GENERAL REPORT relating to Northern Ireland as a whole, covering
in more detail the statistics shown in the County Volumes, and con-
taining in addition tables showing:-
1.
the occupational distribution of persons engaged in each of
51 groups of industries ;
11. the distribution of the foreign born population by nationality,
age, marital condition, and occupation;
iii. the distribution of families of dependent children under 16
years of age, by age, sex, marital condition, and occupation of
parent;
iv. the occupational distribution of person.s suffering from infirmities.
This report also contains particulars of the methods employed in
taking the Census and an Ordnance Survey Map showing the density
of population throughout Northern Ireland.
3.
THIS TOPOGRAPHICAL INDEX.
The net prices for the above publications are 5s. Od. for each County Volume
and lOs. Od. each for the General Report and the Topographical Index.
A complete
set of these volumes ·may be obtained directly from the Superintendent, H.M.
Stationery Office, 15, Donegall Square West, Belfast, at the special inclusive
price of £2 2s. Od. strictly net.
The Registrar-General is empowered by Section 6 (2)' of the Census Act
(Northern Ireland), 1925, to prepare and supply from the Census returns statistics
other than those published in the Census Reports, at the request and cost of any
local authority or person. Any special demands for statistics more detailed than
those published (either as regards subject matter or area) can thus, as far as office
records permit, be satisfied.
Requests for special compilation of Census statistics
should be addressed to the Registrar-General, who will be pleased to advise and
furnish an estimate of cost.
JULY, 1929.
GENERAL REGISTER OFFICE,
MURRAY STREET,
BELFAST.

## Page 179

v
INTRODUCTION.
This volume, the primary object of which is to facilitate reference to the
statistics published in the 1926 Census County Volumes, contains indexes to
the following territorial divisions of N orthem Ireland as they existed at the Census
date.
1.
Parliamentary Constituencies.
2.
Poor Law Unions (or Superintendent Registrars' Districts).
3.
County (i.e., Urban and Rural) Districts.
4.
County Boroughs.
5.
County Electoral Divisions.
6.
Dispensary (or Registrars') Districts.
7.
District Electoral Divisions (including Wards).
8.
Towns.
9.
Townlands.
Complete topographical particulars, with Ordnance Survey and Census County
Volume references, are given for each item in Indexes 2-9.
In addition, Indexes
8-9 contain references to the obsolete territorial units of barony and parish (see
below), and Index 9 shows the area of each townland.
Counties.-Of the six counties in Northern Ireland all but Antrim and Down
(the origin of which is uncertain) were formed in or about the year 1584.
The
largest county is Tyrone, with 779,563 acres ; the smallest, Armagh, with 312,767
acres.
(These acreages exclude land under the larger rivers, lakes and tideways).
Parliamentary Constituencies.-The constituencies shown were, at the date
of the Census, used for the purposes of elections both to the Imperial Parliament
and the Parliament of Northern Ireland. For the latter purpose they have since
been replaced by single-member constituencies formed pursuant to the House of
Commons (Method of Voting and Redistribution of Seats) Act (N.I.), 1929.
Poor Law Unions (or Superintendent Registrars' Districts).-Poor Law Unions
are multiples of townlands, district electoral divisions, and dispensary districts ;
and usually comprise a rural district with the one or more urban districts situate
therein. They were established as the area of administration for poor relief by the
Poor Relief (I) Act, 1838, and were originally constituted by taking a large market
town as a centre and attaching to it the adjoining rural district within a radius
of about ten miles.
Until the passing of the Local Government (I) Act of 1898
poor law guardians levied their own rates and it was therefore unnecessary, when
unions were being delimited, to pay any regard to county boundaries. There are
twenty-seven poor law unions in Northern Ireland, of which twenty are situated
wholly in one county, four extend into two counties, and one into three counties.
Of the remaining two one lies partly in a county and partly in a county borough,
the other partly in two counties and partly in a county borough.
The Superintendent Registrars' Districts formed under the Act 26 Vic., cap. 11,
by which a general registration of births and deaths was established in Ireland, are
in all cases identical with the poor law unions, the clerks of the unions being ex-
officio Superintendent Registrars. The districts of the Registrars of Marriages
under the Act 7. and 8 Vic., cap. 81 are also co-extensive with these districts and the
poor law unions.
County Districts.-These were formed pursuant to the Local Government (I)
Act, 1898, from the sanitary districts established by the Public Health (I) Act,
1878. They consist for the most part of groups of district electoral divisions,*
and are of two classes, urban and rural, each district having a separate council.
Urban district councils are the sole rating authorities within their respective
*In 1926 each of sixteen urban. districts constituted a single district electoral division.


---

## Page 180

vi
jurisdictions, and have full administrative powers "in matters pe~·taining t? public
health. They are also responsible for the maintenance and repa1r of pubh? roads
within their areas; they may formulate and execute schetnes for the hous1ng of
the working-classes; and they are authorised to provide water, gas and electricity
supplies.
Rural district councils have themselves no fiscal powers, though the areas which
they control form the units of taxation for county purposes.
They are primarily
public health authorities, their obligatory an~ p~rmissive .functions in this respect
being virtually as great as those of urban d1stnct counc1ls.
They have also ex-
tensive powers in connection with the erection and supervision of houses for agri-
cultural labourers.
There were in 1926 thirty-one urban districts and thirty-two rural districts
in Northern Ireland. Since that time two urban districts (Bangor and. Coleraine)
have become boroughs, and one town under the Towns llnprovement Act of 1854
(Whitehead) has become an urban district.
County Boroughs.-County boroughs ·were established by the Local Govern-
ment (I) Act, 1898.
These areas are both adn1inistrative counties and urban dis-
tricts, and the councils of the two county boroughs in Northern Ireland therefore
exercise the same powers and perforn1 the same duties as county councils on the
one hand, and urban district councils on the other.
County Electoral Divisions.-These are areas into which each county is divided
for the purpose of election of members of the county council under the Local
Government (I) Act, 1898. They are multiples of district electoral divisions and
are arranged with a view to the population of each division being as nearly as
possible equal. The county electoral divisions in Northern Ireland were recon-
stituted in 1923 and now number 129.
Dispensary (or Registrars') Districts.-Dispensary districts were created under
the prov]sions of the Medical Charities Act, 1851.
They are sub-divisions of poor
law unions, formed with regard to extent and population.
Under the Registration Act of 1863 ·dispensary districts were adopted as
registrars' districts, subject to such alterations as n1ight be deemed expedient. Thus
there were in Northern Ireland at the date of the Census 177 dispensary districts
and 178 registrars' districts, the dispensary district in one case forming two regis-
tration districts.
The present numbers are 178 and 179 respectively.
District Electoral Divisions.-These were originally established under the
Poor Relief (I) Act, 1838 as poor law electoral divisions, their present name being
fixed by the Local Government (I) Act, 1898. The district electoral divisions in
Northern Ireland were reconstituted in 1923 and no-vv number 701.
Each rural
district electoral division consists of a group of townlands.
These areas are used
for the purposes of local government and poor law elections.
Wards are sub-divisions of county boroughs, boroughs and urban districts.
At the date of the Census they existed in 18 of the 33 urban areas, being in all but
one case co-terminous with the urban district electoral divisions.
In the exception
referred to, the urban district, which consists of a single district electoral division,
is divided into two wards.
Towns.-The towns shown comprise those created under the Towl1s Im-
provement (I) Act, 1854-four in nu1nber-together ·with 140 towns of fifty or more
houses having no legally defined boundary.
One of the former class (Whitehead)
has since been constituted an urban district.
Townlands.-Townlands are the units from which all the other territorial
divisions, except those in urban areas, are made up.
They are the smallest
recognised divisions in the Census publications ~nd on the Ordnance Survey maps,


---

## Page 181

vii
and at the date of the Census numbered 9,521.
In size they vary considerably,
but on an average they contain about 350 acres each.
Boundary Alterations.-The Ministry of Home Affairs has statutory power
to alter, by sealed order, the boundaries of the various local government areas re-
ferred to above, subject to the provisos that
(a) Any alteration of a county or borough boundary shall be confirmed
by Parliament.
(b) A union shall not, if it is conveniently possible to avoid it, be divided
between more than two counties, and shall not in any case be divided
between more than three counties.
(c) A county district shall be situate wholly in one county
(d) A district electoral division shall be situate wholly in one county
district.
(e) So far as practicable, the boundaries of an area of local government
shall not intersect the boundaries of any other area of local govern-
ment.
Changes in townland boundaries rendered necessary by the straightening of
river courses, reclamation from the sea, or other circumstance, are made on the
Ordnance Survey maps after approval by the Chief Boundary Surveyor.
Details of all boundary changes in administrative areas between 1911 and
1926 (apart from thos·e occasioned by the reconstitution of county and district
electoral divisions in 1923) will be found in the Census County Volumes.
LISTS OF BARONIES AND PARISHES.
In order to meet the needs of persons who rna y wish to ascertain the barony
and civil parish in which any given townland is situate, lists of these now obsolete
terntorial divisions, with,numerical references corresponding to those given in the
last two columns of the Townland Index, are appended.
The barony was originally a feudal and military jurisdiction, but came in course
of time to be used for fiscal and administrative purposes only.
The parish, at first
a purely ecclesiastical division, was adopted at an early date as an area for civil
purposes also ; but even before the passing of the Irish Church Act of 1869 the
boundaries of the civil and ecclesiastical parishes were not in all cases identical.

THE QUEEN'S

## Page 341

iii.
PREFACE .
. This volume has been prepared in accordance vvith the provisions of
Section 6 (1) of the Census Act (Northern Ireland), 1925. The 1926 Census
statistics which it contains vvere con1piled from the returns made as at midnight

    of the 18-19th April, 1926: they supersede those in the Preliminary Report
published in August, 1926, and may be regarded as final.
~
The Census publications vvi.ll consist of:-
1.
SEVEN CouNTY VoLUMES, each similar in design and scope to the present
publication.
u
2.
A GENERAL REPORT relating to Northern Ireland as a whole, covering
in more detail the statistics shown in the County Volumes, and
containing in addition tables sho\ving
(i.) the occupational distribution of persons engaged In each of
51 groups of industries ;
(ii.) the distribution of the foreign born population by nationality,
age, marital condition, and occupation;
(iii.) the distribution of families of dependent children under 16
years of age, by age, sex, marital condition, and occupation of
parent;
(iv.) the occupational distribution of persons suffering from infirmities.
3.
A TOPOGRAPHICAL INDEX, dealing in separate alphabetical sections with
each type of area for which statistics are published in the County
series.
The particulars given for each territorial unit include references
to the 6" Ordnance Survey maps and to Tables 3, 4, or 5 of the
relative County Volume.
The net prices for the above publications will be 5s. Od. for each County
Volume and lOs. Od. each for the General Report and the Topographical Index.
A complete set of these volumes may be
obtained directly from the
Superintendent, H.M. Stationery O:.tice, 15, Donegall Square vVest, Belfast, at the
special inclusive price of £2 2s. Od. strictly net.
The Registrar-General is empo\vered by Section 6 (2) of the Census Act
(Northern Ireland), 1925, to prepare and supply from the Census returns, statistics
other than those published in the Census Reports at the request and cost of any
local authority or person. Any special demands for statistics more detailed than
those published (either as regards subject matter or area) can thus, as far as office
records permit, be satisfied.
Requests for special compilation of Census statistics
should be addressed to the Registrar-General, who \Vill be pleased to advise and
furnish an estimate of cost.
FEBRUARY, 1928.
GENERAL REGISTER OFFICE,
lVIuRRAY STREET,
BELFAST.

## Page 344

vi.
PART I.
DEFINITIONS AND EXPLANATIONS OF
TERMS USED IN THIS VOLUME.
AREA.
The acreages given are as measured by the Ordnance Survey ?f 1899-1907
(fore-shore being excluded), and relate to the several areas as constituted at the
date of the 1926 Census. The acreage covered by tidal water, ·which is not sho\vn
in the body of Tables 3 and 4, appears in footnotes thereto.
POPULATION.
The population comprises all persons, including Forces of the Cro·wn, enumer-

    ated as at midnight of Sunday the 18th-19th April, 1926, together vvith those who,
not having been enumerated elsewhere, arrived in port or \Yere received into any
dwe~ing on the morning of the 19th April tefore the Census returns \Vere collected.
The population assigned to each area is that actually present (as defined
above) "Within it, whether as permanent residents or temporary visitors, persons
in port but on ship board being classified to the adjacent area.
In general these definitions apply to the population as enumerated at previous
censuses, and shown in Table 2.
ADMINISTRATIVE
DIVISIONS.
The County Borough is divided into fifteen wards, each of which is
coterminous with the district electoral division of the same name (see Table 3).
The County Borough covers only a part of the Poor Law Union of Belfast,
the remaining parts consisting of the Rural Districts of Belfast and Castlereagh
and the Urban District of Holywood. The part of the Poor Lavv Union within
the County Borough is divided into sixteen medical dispensary districts (see
Table 4).
Poor law unions and medical dispensary districts are, respectively,
superintendent registrars' and registrars' districts for the registration of marriat,es,
births, and deaths.
The County Borough forms a single regional education area (see Table 12).
The only changes which have taken place in the boundaries of adminis-

    trative divisions since 1911 are the creation in 1920 of Dispensary District No. 16
out of parts of Districts Nos. 11 and 12, and the transfer at the same time of

    portions of District No. 13,to Districts Nos. 11 and 12.
PARLIAMENTARY CONSTITUENCIES.
The present parliamentary constituencies were created by the Government
of Ireland Act, 1920, by grouping the then existing parliamentary divisions.
'The· wards contained in each constituency are shown in a footnote to Table III.
HOUSES.
, "'l. The term ;' House ~' denotes
.".(a) each structure (except ins~itutions) which stands alone or is separated
from another by a vertical party wall; and which is used or intended
partly or wholly for habitation ;
(b) each institution (viz., hos:pi!al, sanatorh:m, nursing home, infirmary,
workhouse, asylum, rehgwus or chantable community, residential
scf1ool or college,. barr~c~s .or oth~r premises under Naval, Military,
A1r Force, or Pohce dlsciphn~, pnson. or. o~her pl~ce of detention),
regardless of the number of buildings w1th1n 1ts prec1ncts;
(c) each vessel1 caravan, t~nt 1 or other movable dwelling.


---

## Page 345

Vll .
•
1 ~he l~l~ figures fo~ "Houses'~ d~ n~t include vessels, and each separate
btn1.ding within the precincts of an Institution was at that Census regarded as a
separate "House."
2.
The terms "Boarding-House" and "Lodging-House" denote, in addition
to habitations so described, any private house containing three or more boarders
or lodgers.
3.
The tern1 "Private I-Iouse" denotes all habitations other than
(a) dwellings \\rithin the precincts of an institution;
(b) hotels, boarding houses, and other such communal dwellings;
(c) premises in which three or more boarders, lodgers, or trade assistants
reside;
(d) residences occupied by virtue of the tenant's employment, (except
rectories and manses, and dwellings belonging to but detached
from domestic establishments) ;
(e) premise$ occupied by caretakers;
(f)
ships, caravans, tents, and other movable d\vellings;
(g) barns, stables, and out-houses occupied on Census night.
The term includes d\vellings over shops except when these are occupied by
a business manager (see (d) above).
4.
The term " Inhabited " denotes actual residence of a person or persons,
on Census night.
" Uninhabited " houses, therefore, include those temporarily
vacant through the absence of the usual occupiers as well as those without tenants.
ROOMS.
I. The rooms enumerated are the usual living rooms, including bedrooms
and kitchens, but excluding sculleries (i.e., rooms used only for washing, cleaning,
or cooking), pantries, cloakrooms, landings, closets or bathrooms, or any ware-
house, office or shop rooms.
2.
For the purpose of classify1.ng families by rooms occupied (Table 8),
shared-room occupations-i.e., those in which two or more families have
rooms in com1non,-have been rounded up to the next integral number of rooms.
Thus, three families occupying 1-§-, .2-§-, and 2j rooms in a 6-roomed house \vould
be shown as occupying 2, 3, and 3 rooms respectively.
The extent of the
rounding-up process may be ascertained in a general way by reference to Table 7,
in which the actual number of rooms is given. Generally, in cases of 1 room
occupations, all persons living therein, whether related or not, have been enumer-
ated as one Census family.
FAMILIES.
The term "Family" denotes
(a) The inhabitants of each institution (see l (b) of " Houses " definition),
hotel, boarding-house, lodging-house, or ship.
(b) Each person or group of persons occupying separately the whole, or part
of, any dwelling other than as described at (a).
'' Private Families " mean all families enumerated in " Private Houses " (see
3 of '' Houses " definition) ; and from the population and family totals of this
class is calculated the number of persons per family in 1926 (See Table VI.).
In 1911 the inhabitants of premises described at (a) above ·were in certain
cases enumerated as two or more families. The number of persons per family
was obtained by dividing the number of Census families into the entire population
less the " special inm:ttes" (i.e., patients, prisoners, soldiers, etc.) of institutions.


---

## Page 346

viii.
RATEABLE
VALUATION.
The figures shown are those for service of the year ended :31st l\1arch, 1~27,
and have been supplied by the Commissioner of Valuation.
Rateable hered~ta­
ments include lands, buildings, tolls, raihvays, tram-roads, canals, fishenes,
turbaries, mines, quarries, waterworks, telegraphs, and t~lephones. The figures
for individual wards shown in Table 3 exclude the valuation of electnc, gas, and
water mains, tramways, telegraphs, and telephones, it not being practicable to
allocate valuations of this type between the different wards.
EDUCATION.
The classification under educational establishments (which was assisted
by reference to lists supplied for Census purposes by the Ministry of Education)
is as follows :-
l. PUBLIC ELEMENTARY SCHOOLS.
Day and evening pupils are grouped
together in this class, the question on the Census schedule not
requiring a separation as between day and evening in respect of
pupils attending public elementary schools.
2.
SECONDARY ScHOOLS, including preparatory departments and preparatory
schools.
This heading relates to day pupils only, and the schools
are limited to those recognised by the lVIinistry of Education.
3.
TECHNICAL ScHooLs. The day pupils shown in this group comprise those
stated to be attending junior technical schools, day commercial
classes, and day classes for domestic economy and manual instruction
which were being held at certain centres in April, 1926.
The evening
pupils comprise those stated to be attending technical schools or
classes in the evening.
4.
OTHER EDUCATIONAL ESTABLISHMENTS (Day and Evening).
These in-
clude secondary schools not recognised by the 1\Enistry of Education,
universities, training colleges for teachers, agricultural schools,
commercial schools, home or private tuition, etc.
OCCUPATIONS.
The occupational classification is based on that used in the Reports on the
Census of England and Wales, 1921. *
It will be seen that there are thirty-two orders.
Each of these covers the
same occupational ground as the England and \Vales orders.
Eleven of the
orders are divided into thirty-two sub-orders, each of which is comparable with
the corresponding England and Wales sub-order, with the following exceptions :-

ix.
INDUSTRIES.
The industrial classification is based on that used in the Reports on the
Census of England and Wales, 1921. *
I~ vvill b: seen that there are twenty-hvo orders. Each of these covers the
same 1ndustnal ground as the England and \Vales orders.
Nine of the orders
are divide~ into thirty-eight sub-orders, each of vvhich is comparable \vith the
corresponding England and \Vales sub-order, with the following exceptions:-

Within the sub-orders are 226 industrial headings, evolved by processes

BIRTHPLACES.
Persons stated to have been born in Ireland-the County name or
"Irish Free State" not being inserted-are assumed to have been born in the
Irish Free State. Where nationality is given but birthplace is not, it has been
assumed that the person was born in the country of which he or she is a national.
All persons of British nationality and unstated birthplace have been regarded
as born in the County of enumeration.
RELIGIOUS PROFESSIONS.
The religious professions, other than Roman Catholic, Presbyterian, Church
of Ireland, and Methodist, have been classified under the headings set out at foot
of Table 18, separate mention being made of all professions containing 10 or more
adherents. The heading "Presbyterian" includes those who call themselves
by this name without qualification, together \vith those returned as members of
the Established Church of Scotland.
Other Presbyterian denominations are
shown at the foot of the table.
Members of the Church of England and the
~Episcopal Church of Scotland have not been included under Church of Ireland
except where stated. The latter group includes all persons returned as "Church"
or " Episcopalian."
ORPHANHOOD.
IR compiling statistics under this head, step-parents, foster-parents, and
adoptive parents, so stated, have been treated in the same manner as natural
parents.· Where the information as to one parent is stated as "not known,"
that parent has been assumed to be dead and the case dealt with accordingly.
Where information as to neither parent is given, the case has been placed in the
"No Statement" group.
INFIRMITIES.
1.
DEAF AND DUMB, DuMB, BLIND. The figures given are in respect of
persons so returned, except where the answers indicate only partial infirmity as
"nearly blind."
* Full details of the English system of classification are published in the "Classification of Industries,
Census, 1921," to be purchased directly from H.M.S.O., or th!'ough any Bookseller.


---

## Page 348

X.
2.
CRIPPLED.
In addition to those persons
specificall~y described as
"Crippled," the figures given include all cases in which it is clearly indicated
that the person
(a) has suffered a total pern1anent loss of the use of arm, hand, leg, or
foot;
(b) is pern1anently crippled in such a degree as to prevent the free
pursuit of n1ost ordinary occupations.
The following cases, treated as "Crippled," indicate the variety of the terms
used in answering the question on the Census schedule:-
Arm-defonned, disabled; hand-deformed; hip joint disease; knee-
decayed, tubercular arthritis in; leg-disabled, diseased bone in,
infinn, short; on crutch; paralysis-infantile, nervous, of arms and
side, of legs and back, of spine; rheumatoid arthritis; spinal caries;
unable to walk.
3.
MENTALLY AFFLICTED.
All cases returned, whatever the degree of
affliction, are included in the tables. The following terms were used in the
returns:-
Afflicted; feeble minded; imbecile ; melancholia ; mental debility ; simple ;
weak intellect.
4.
Persons suffering from more than one infirmity have been classified to
that which appears most likely to constitute the greater, or greatest, disability,
infirmities being graded for this purpose from greatest to least, thus:-
Mentally afflicted; blind; crippled; deaf and dumb; dumb.
CONVENTION
AS
TO
DOTS
(.)
AND
DASHES (-).
Throughout this volume dots(.) are used in tabular matter to indicate nil (0),
and dashes ( -) are used to indicate that information cannot or is not intended
to be given.
CORRECTION
OF CENSUS
DATA.
Every effort has been made to eliminate, as far as practicable, errors and
inaccuracies from the Census data. The process of correction began with the
Enumerators, who received full instructions as to the action to be taken in cases
of error or omission; it was continued by a system of check applied at each
stage of the office work ; and was completed by an intensive scrutiny and inter-
comparison of the tabulation sheets.
This scrutiny and comparison revealed
a certain number of incongruities, which, when material, were investigated and
corrected in the light of the facts disclosed.
Errors of a minor nature (i.e.,
such as could not affect the statistical value of the Census tables) were adjusted
more or less arbitrarily.

## Page 349

xi.
PART II.
STATISTICAL NOTES.
The definitions, etc. contained in Part I of this volume apply \Vith equal
force to the contents of these notes and to the tables contained in Part III. Roman
numerals are used in this volume for the tables in Part II., and Arabic numerals
for the tables in Part III.
AREA.
. The area of ~he Borough of Belfast, \vhich in 1851 vvas roughly 2i square
m1les, no\v compnses (as a result of the "boundary extensions in 1853 and 1896)
some 23 square miles, exclusive of tidal \Vater.
It ·will te noted from Table I
that vvithin the Borough boundary there are almost 1li square miles of land
under crops and pasture, as against 10-! square miles covered by buildings and
roads.
POPULATION.
The population numbers 415,151 persons, having doubled in the past 45
years and being nearly six times as great as that enumerated in 1841. The increase
over the past 15 years however, only amounts to 7.3 per cent., \vhich is the smallest
percentage increase yet recorded. The actual increase is 28,204 persons, which
is sn1aller than any decennial increase recorded since 1851.
(Figures for the earlier
periods are shown in Table 2.)

(a) Owing to the e1Ctension of the Borough boundary in 1896, and the fact that registration areas did not
coincide with the area of the Borough prior to 1899, it is not practicable to show figures in this table
for periods prior to 1901.
(b) Including the deaths of non-ch·ilians of Belfast County Borough which occurred outside Northern Ireland.
It has not been found possible to estimate the number of these deaths at all closely, but from the
War mortality lists of soldiers from Ireland, which were compiled by the War Office from regimental
records, it appears that for the whole of Northern Ireland the non-civilian deaths which occurred outside the
country numbered about 10,000 during the last intercensal period, exclusive of the deaths of regular
soldiers stationed outside the country at the date of the 1911 Census.
While comparable figures of natural increase due to the excess of births over
deaths are not available for periods prior to 1901 (see footnote (a) to Table I),
it is evident that the rapid increase in the population from 1821 to 1901 was in
part due to considerable gains from net movements into the City. This augmen-
tation of natural increase was arrested over the period 1901 to 1911, \vhen the
outward movement of population almost exactly balanced the influx, and in the
last intercensal period we find the net movement converted into such a substantial
outward one as materially to offset the increase in population which would other-

wise have been experienced from the excess o~ births over de~ths. In this
connection account should be taken of the increasing tendency of City workers to
live in the country or at the seaside. Evidence of this tenden~y ~s app3:re~t from
the present trend of population in those urban and rural distncts ·within easy
reach of Belfast.

* Exclusive of I,723 acres tidal water.
From Table II it is seen that fourteen out of the fifteen wards of the County
Borough show increases of population during the past 15 years, but the average
rate of increase per annum is considerably less in all the wards, except Court,
Dock, and Smithfield, than during the preceding intercensal period of ten years.
It is interesting to note that, according to the persons per acre rates, these three
are amongst the four most thickly populated wards in the County Borough. Such
density rates, however, should only be used ,with reserve in measuring the degree
of" overcrowding" in urban areas, since the acreages of these areas may include
widely differing proportions of waste land, land under pasture, etc. The greatest
actual increase in population during the past 15 years was in Ormeau Ward,
and the greatest percentage increase W9-s in. Falls

In. St. George's
Ward there was a small decrease!

Tables 3 and 4 provide more detailed information regarding the population
in the various sub-divisions of the County Borough and for convenience Table 3
incorporates figures of rateable valuation. The rateable valuation has increased

    from £1,527,932 to £1,793,563 over the past :fifteen years, an increase of 17 per
cent. as compared with the 7 per cent. increase of population recorded for the
County Borough.

NOTE.-The present Parliamentary Divisions were created by the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, and
are constituted as follows :-
EAST BELFAST. -Dock, Pottinger, and Victoria Wards.
NoRTH BELFAST. -Clifton, Duncairn, and Shankill \Vards.
SoUTH BELFAST.
Cromac, Ormeau, and Windsor \Vards.
WEsT BELFAST. ·-Court, Falls, St. Anne's, St. George's, Smithfield, and Woodvale \Vards.
Parliamentary Constituencies.-In considering the percentages sho\vn in
Table III, regard should be given to the qualifications entitling a person to be
registered as an elector.
In general, a man is qualified if 21 years of age or up\vards,
with the requisite residence or business premises qualification : a woman, if 30
years of age or upwards, and if (I) entitled to be registered as a local government
elector in respect .of the occupation of land or premises (not being a dwelling-
house) of a yearly value of not less than £5, or of a dwelling-house ; or (2) the wife
of a husband entitled to be so registered.
It may be noted thttt a person may be registered as a parliamentary elector
1n a constituency although not resident therein.
The question of the comparison of the number of parliamentary electors
with population will be dealt with more fully in the General Report.

* The figures given are for the Borough as constituted at the date of each Census.
The boundary of the
Borough was extended in 1853 and again in 1806.
The total number of houses in the County Borough increased by 5.5 per cent.
since 1911, as compared with the increase in population of 7.3 per cent. There
has, however, been a considerable reduction in the number of uninhabited houses,
the 1926 figure for which is the smallest recorded since 1861. The resulting
position is that the number of inhabited houses has increased since 1911 by
practically 10 per cent., i.e., a figure appreciably in excess of the population
Increase.
The number of houses under construction was more than twice as many as

    in 1911, but appreciably less than in 1881, 1891, and 1901.
Persons per Inhabited House and Persons per Family.-The average number
of persons per inhabited house, which showed a steady decrease from 1851 to

    1911, has again shown a decrease from 1911 to 1926.
The average number of
persons per family, which was practically constant for the period 1881-1911
has also shown a marked fall.
In this connection it should be remembered that
the Census family (defined on page vii) is not to be confused with the natural
family of parents and children.
The figures of the number of families per 100 inhabited houses at earlier census

    enumerations commencing with 1871 are as follows, 131, 119, 110, 104 and 103
    in 1911, whilst for 1926 the figure is 109. In this respect therefore there has



---

## Page 353

XV.
apparently been a slight retrograde movement since 1911-a result to be expected
from the fact that. the p~oportion of families to population has increased at a greater
rate than that of Inhabited houses to population.

Housing in Wards.-Table V shovvs that in ten of the wards there has been
anincrease in the total numl:er of houses and in :five a decrease. Comparison of
this table with Table II also shows that the changes in houses, as compared with
population, reveal the greatest divergencies in the Smithfield and Court Wards.
In these cases, while the population increased by 5.7 per cent. and 6.5 per cent.
respectively, the numbers of houses decreased by 10.5 per cent. and 8.6 per cent.
. The extent of sub-letting in the various wards is best indicated by the factor
"number of private families per inhabited private house." This factor is readily
obtainable from the :figures of house accommodation contained in Table 7, and it
is seen that the number of families per 100 private houses is 109 for the County
Borough, and that this rate varies between 125 for the Smithfield Ward and 105
for the Ormeau and Windsor Wards. Sub-letting is thus most prevalent in the
Smithfield Ward where 22 per cent, or, roughly, 1 in 5, of the houses sub-let or take
in ~odgers who do not board with the family.
Of all the inhabited private houses in the County Borough 92 per cent. are
occupied by single families_a!!d 63 per-cent. contain 4 to 5 rooms.
